title: New York City Subway

infobox.value: [City of New York](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City")

[36](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_lines "List of New York City Subway lines") lines[[note 1]](#cite_note-1) [28](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_services "List of New York City Subway services") services (1 planned)[[note 2]](#cite_note-2)

[472](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_stations "List of New York City Subway stations")[[1]](#cite_note-ridership2014-3) ([MTA](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") total count)[[note 3]](#cite_note-planned_stations-7)[[note 4]](#cite_note-stationnote-9) 423 stations if station complexes are counted as one station[[note 4]](#cite_note-stationnote-9)[[1]](#cite_note-ridership2014-3) (per [international standards](/wiki/List_of_metro_systems "List of metro systems")) 17 planned[[note 3]](#cite_note-planned_stations-7)

October 27, 1904; 121 years ago (1904-10-27) ([Original subway](/wiki/Early_history_of_the_IRT_subway "Early history of the IRT subway"))  July 3, 1868; 157 years ago (1868-07-03)[[7]](#cite_note-12) (first elevated, rapid transit operation) October 9, 1863; 162 years ago (1863-10-09) (first railroad operation)[[note 6]](#cite_note-13)
[New York City Transit Authority](/wiki/New_York_City_Transit_Authority "New York City Transit Authority") (NYCTA)

248 mi (399 km)[[10]](#cite_note-mtabudget-16)      (route length) 665 mi (1,070 km)[[10]](#cite_note-mtabudget-16)      (track length, revenue) 850 mi (1,370 km)[[11]](#cite_note-NYCSubway.org-17)      (track length, total)

A [1](/wiki/1_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "1 (New York City Subway service)") local train made up of ten [Bombardier R62A](/wiki/R62A_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R62A (New York City Subway car)") cars enters the [207th Street Station](/wiki/207th_Street_station "207th Street station") of the [IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line "IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line"). **Bottom**: An [E](/wiki/E_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "E (New York City Subway service)") local train made up of ten [Alstom R160A](/wiki/R160_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R160 (New York City Subway car)") cars leaves the [Queens Plaza station](/wiki/Queens_Plaza_station "Queens Plaza station") of the [IND Queens Boulevard Line](/wiki/IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line "IND Queens Boulevard Line"). | |

is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), serving four of the city's five [boroughs](/wiki/New_York_City_boroughs "New York City boroughs"): [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"). It is owned by the [government of New York City](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City") and leased to the [New York City Transit Authority](/wiki/New_York_City_Transit_Authority "New York City Transit Authority"),[[14]](#cite_note-Hood,_Clifton_2004-20) an affiliate agency of the [state](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_(state) "Government of New York (state)")-run [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") (MTA).[[15]](#cite_note-21) Opened on October 27, 1904, the New York City Subway is one of the world's oldest public transit systems, one of the most-used, and the one with the [most stations](/wiki/List_of_metro_systems "List of metro systems"),[[16]](#cite_note-22) with [472 stations in operation](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_stations "New York City Subway stations")[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23) (423, if stations connected by transfers are counted as single stations).[[1]](#cite_note-ridership2014-3)

The **New York City Subway** is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), serving four of the city's five [boroughs](/wiki/New_York_City_boroughs "New York City boroughs"): [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"). It is owned by the [government of New York City](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City") and leased to the [New York City Transit Authority](/wiki/New_York_City_Transit_Authority "New York City Transit Authority"),[[14]](#cite_note-Hood,_Clifton_2004-20) an affiliate agency of the [state](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_(state) "Government of New York (state)")-run [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") (MTA).[[15]](#cite_note-21) Opened on October 27, 1904, the New York City Subway is one of the world's oldest public transit systems, one of the most-used, and the one with the [most stations](/wiki/List_of_metro_systems "List of metro systems"),[[16]](#cite_note-22) with [472 stations in operation](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_stations "New York City Subway stations")[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23) (423, if stations connected by transfers are counted as single stations).[[1]](#cite_note-ridership2014-3)

The system has operated [24/7 service](/wiki/24/7_service "24/7 service") every day of the year throughout most of its history, barring emergencies and disasters. By annual ridership, the New York City Subway is the busiest rapid transit system in both the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere") and the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), as well as the [ninth-busiest rapid transit rail system](/wiki/List_of_metro_systems "List of metro systems") in the world.[[18]](#cite_note-24) The subway carried 2,314,388,000 unlinked, non-unique riders in 2025.[[6]](#cite_note-APTA_Ridership_Report-10): 2[[note 5]](#cite_note-ridershipnote-11) Daily ridership has been calculated since 1985; the record, over 6.2 million, was set on October 29, 2015.[[20]](#cite_note-26)

The system is also one of the world's longest. Overall, the system consists of 248 miles (399 km) of routes,[[10]](#cite_note-mtabudget-16) comprising a total of 665 miles (1,070 km) of revenue track[[10]](#cite_note-mtabudget-16) and a total of 850 miles (1,370 km) including [non-revenue trackage](/wiki/Non-revenue_track "Non-revenue track").[[11]](#cite_note-NYCSubway.org-17) Of the system's 28 routes or "services" (which usually share track or "lines" with other services), 25 pass through Manhattan, the exceptions being the [G](/wiki/G_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "G (New York City Subway service)") train, the [Franklin Avenue Shuttle](/wiki/Franklin_Avenue_Shuttle "Franklin Avenue Shuttle"), and the [Rockaway Park Shuttle](/wiki/Rockaway_Park_Shuttle "Rockaway Park Shuttle"). Large portions of the subway outside Manhattan are elevated, on [embankments](/wiki/Embankment_(transportation) "Embankment (transportation)"), or in [open cuts](/wiki/Cut_(earthmoving) "Cut (earthmoving)"), and a few stretches of track run at ground level; 40% of track is above ground.[[21]](#cite_note-27) Many lines and stations have both [express](/wiki/Express_train "Express train") and local services. These lines have three or four tracks. Normally, the outer two are used by local trains, while the inner one or two are used by express trains.[[11]](#cite_note-NYCSubway.org-17)

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), the New York City Subway's budgetary burden for expenditures was $8.7 billion, supported by collection of fares, bridge tolls, and [earmarked](/wiki/Earmark_(finance) "Earmark (finance)") regional taxes and fees, as well as direct funding from state and local governments.[[22]](#cite_note-28)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the New York City Subway](/wiki/History_of_the_New_York_City_Subway "History of the New York City Subway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/King%27s_Color-graphs_of_New_York_City11.jpg/250px-King%27s_Color-graphs_of_New_York_City11.jpg)](/wiki/File:King%27s_Color-graphs_of_New_York_City11.jpg)

Some old pictures from the New York City Subway (1910)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/City_Hall_Subway_station.jpg/250px-City_Hall_Subway_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_Hall_Subway_station.jpg)

The [City Hall station](/wiki/City_Hall_station_(IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line) "City Hall station (IRT Lexington Avenue Line)") of the [IRT Lexington Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line "IRT Lexington Avenue Line"), part of the [first underground line of the subway](/wiki/Early_history_of_the_IRT_subway "Early history of the IRT subway") that opened on October 27, 1904

[Alfred Ely Beach](/wiki/Alfred_Ely_Beach "Alfred Ely Beach") built the first demonstration for an underground transit system in New York City in 1869 and opened it in February 1870.[[23]](#cite_note-Brennan-29)[[24]](#cite_note-30) His [Beach Pneumatic Transit](/wiki/Beach_Pneumatic_Transit "Beach Pneumatic Transit") only extended 312 feet (95 m) under [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") operating from Warren Street to Murray Street[[23]](#cite_note-Brennan-29) and exhibited his idea for an [atmospheric railway](/wiki/Atmospheric_railway "Atmospheric railway") as a subway. The tunnel was never extended for political and financial reasons.[[25]](#cite_note-31) Today, no part of this line remains as the tunnel was completely within the limits of the present-day [City Hall station](/wiki/City_Hall_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "City Hall (BMT Broadway Line)") under Broadway.[[26]](#cite_note-32)[[27]](#cite_note-33)[[28]](#cite_note-nyt1996-34)[[29]](#cite_note-nyt_201308-35) The [Great Blizzard of 1888](/wiki/Great_Blizzard_of_1888 "Great Blizzard of 1888") helped demonstrate the benefits of an underground transportation system.[[30]](#cite_note-36) A plan for the construction of the subway was approved in 1894, and construction began in 1900.[[31]](#cite_note-PBS-37) Even though the underground portions of the subway had yet to be built, several above-ground segments of the modern-day New York City Subway system were already in service by then. The oldest structure still in use opened in 1885 as part of the [BMT Lexington Avenue Line](/wiki/BMT_Lexington_Avenue_Line "BMT Lexington Avenue Line") in Brooklyn[[32]](#cite_note-Gates-38)[[33]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Daily_Eagle1885-39)[[34]](#cite_note-Chauncey-40)[[35]](#cite_note-MBC-41)[[36]](#cite_note-Alabama-42) and is now part of the [BMT Jamaica Line](/wiki/BMT_Jamaica_Line "BMT Jamaica Line").[[37]](#cite_note-NYCSorg-BMTJamaica-43) The oldest right-of-way, which is part of the [BMT West End Line](/wiki/BMT_West_End_Line "BMT West End Line") near [Coney Island Creek](/wiki/Coney_Island_Creek "Coney Island Creek"), was in use in 1864 as a steam railroad called the [Brooklyn, Bath and Coney Island Rail Road](/wiki/Brooklyn,_Bath_and_Coney_Island_Rail_Road "Brooklyn, Bath and Coney Island Rail Road").[[38]](#cite_note-44)[[39]](#cite_note-45)[[40]](#cite_note-46)

The [first underground line of the subway](/wiki/Early_history_of_the_IRT_subway "Early history of the IRT subway") opened on October 27, 1904, almost 36 years after the opening of the first elevated line in New York City (which became the [IRT Ninth Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Ninth_Avenue_Line "IRT Ninth Avenue Line")).[[41]](#cite_note-Walker1918-47)[[42]](#cite_note-nycsubway-48) The 9.1-mile (14.6 km) subway line, then called the "Manhattan Main Line", ran from [City Hall station](/wiki/City_Hall_station_(IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line) "City Hall station (IRT Lexington Avenue Line)") northward under [Lafayette Street](/wiki/Lafayette_Street "Lafayette Street") (then named Elm Street) and [Park Avenue](/wiki/Park_Avenue "Park Avenue") (then named Fourth Avenue) before turning westward at [42nd Street](/wiki/42nd_Street_(Manhattan) "42nd Street (Manhattan)"). It then curved northward again at [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square"), continuing under [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)") before terminating at [145th Street station](/wiki/145th_Street_station_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "145th Street station (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") in [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem").[[43]](#cite_note-49) Its operation was leased to the [Interborough Rapid Transit Company](/wiki/Interborough_Rapid_Transit_Company "Interborough Rapid Transit Company") (IRT), and over 150,000 passengers[[44]](#cite_note-50) paid the 5-cent fare ($2 in 2025 dollars [[45]](#cite_note-inflation-US-51)) to ride it on the first day of operation.[[46]](#cite_note-52)

By the late 1900s and early 1910s, the lines had been consolidated into two privately owned systems, the IRT and the [Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company](/wiki/Brooklyn_Rapid_Transit_Company "Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company") (BRT, later [Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation"), BMT). The city built most of the lines and leased them to the companies.[[47]](#cite_note-53) The first line of the city-owned and operated [Independent Subway System](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System") (IND) opened in 1932.[[48]](#cite_note-chambers-54) This system was intended to compete with the private systems and allow some of the elevated railways to be torn down but stayed within the core of the city due to its small startup capital.[[14]](#cite_note-Hood,_Clifton_2004-20) This required it to be run 'at cost', necessitating fares up to double the five-cent fare of the time, or 10¢ ($4 in 2025 dollars [[45]](#cite_note-inflation-US-51)).[[49]](#cite_note-Feinman2000-55)

In 1940, the city bought the two private systems. Some elevated lines ceased service immediately while others closed soon after.[[50]](#cite_note-56) Integration was slow, but [several connections](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_inter-division_connections "List of New York City Subway inter-division connections") were built between the IND and BMT.[[51]](#cite_note-57)[[52]](#cite_note-NYTimes-ChrystieStChanges-1967-58)[[53]](#cite_note-Raskin-RoutesNotTaken-20132-59) These now operate as one division, called the [B Division](/wiki/B_Division_(New_York_City_Subway) "B Division (New York City Subway)"). Since the former IRT tunnels are narrower, have sharper curves, and shorter station platforms, they cannot accommodate B Division cars, and the former IRT remains its own division, the [A Division](/wiki/A_Division_(New_York_City_Subway) "A Division (New York City Subway)").[[54]](#cite_note-60) Many passenger transfers between stations of all three former companies have been created, allowing the entire network to be treated as a single unit.[[55]](#cite_note-Sparberg2014-61)

During the late 1940s, the system recorded high ridership, and on December 23, 1946, the system-wide record of 8,872,249 fares was set.[[56]](#cite_note-62): 73

The [New York City Transit Authority](/wiki/New_York_City_Transit_Authority "New York City Transit Authority") (NYCTA), a public authority of New York City, was created in 1953 to take over subway, bus, and streetcar operations from the city, and placed under control of the state-level [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") in 1968.[[55]](#cite_note-Sparberg2014-61)[[57]](#cite_note-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Heavily_tagged_subway_car_in_NY.jpg/250px-Heavily_tagged_subway_car_in_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heavily_tagged_subway_car_in_NY.jpg)

[Graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti") became a notable symbol of declining service during the 1970s.

Organized in 1934 by transit workers of the BRT, IRT, and IND,[[58]](#cite_note-64) the [Transport Workers Union of America](/wiki/Transport_Workers_Union_of_America "Transport Workers Union of America") Local 100 remains the largest and most influential local of the labor unions.[[59]](#cite_note-65) Since the union's founding, there have been three union strikes over contract disputes with the MTA:[[60]](#cite_note-66) 12 days in [1966](/wiki/1966_New_York_City_transit_strike "1966 New York City transit strike"),[[61]](#cite_note-67) 11 days in [1980](/wiki/1980_New_York_City_transit_strike "1980 New York City transit strike"),[[62]](#cite_note-68) and three days in [2005](/wiki/2005_New_York_City_transit_strike "2005 New York City transit strike").[[63]](#cite_note-69)[[64]](#cite_note-70)

By the 1970s and 1980s, the New York City Subway was at an all-time low.[[65]](#cite_note-71)[[66]](#cite_note-nycsubway_1980s-72) Ridership had dropped to 1910s levels, and graffiti and crime were rampant. Maintenance was poor, and delays and track problems were common. Still, the NYCTA managed to open six new subway stations in the 1980s,[[67]](#cite_note-Lorch-73)[[68]](#cite_note-nytimes2-74) make the current fleet of subway cars graffiti-free, as well as order 1,775 new subway cars.[[69]](#cite_note-75) By the early 1990s, conditions had improved significantly, although maintenance backlogs accumulated during those 20 years are still being fixed today.[[66]](#cite_note-nycsubway_1980s-72)

[![The Cortlandt Street station is seen partially collapsed](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/NYCTA-Miller%2CG_CD1-043.jpg/250px-NYCTA-Miller%2CG_CD1-043.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYCTA-Miller,G_CD1-043.jpg)

The [Cortlandt Street station](/wiki/Cortlandt_Street_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "Cortlandt Street (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") partially collapsed as a result of the [collapse of the World Trade Center](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_World_Trade_Center "Collapse of the World Trade Center").

Entering the 21st century, progress continued despite several disasters. The [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") resulted in [service disruptions](/wiki/Closings_and_cancellations_following_the_September_11_attacks#NYC_Subway "Closings and cancellations following the September 11 attacks") on lines running through Lower Manhattan, particularly the [IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line "IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line"), which ran directly underneath the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)").[[70]](#cite_note-USDOT-76) Sections of the tunnel, as well as the [Cortlandt Street](/wiki/Cortlandt_Street_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "Cortlandt Street (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") station, which was directly underneath the Twin Towers, were severely damaged. Rebuilding required the suspension of service on that line south of Chambers Street. Ten other nearby stations were closed for cleanup. By March 2002, seven of those stations had reopened. Except for Cortlandt Street, the rest reopened in September 2002, along with service south of Chambers Street.[[71]](#cite_note-Kennedy-77)[[72]](#cite_note-78)[[73]](#cite_note-Brian_Abbott_2004-79) Cortlandt Street reopened in September 2018.[[74]](#cite_note-80)

In October 2012, [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Effects_of_Hurricane_Sandy_in_New_York "Effects of Hurricane Sandy in New York") flooded several underwater tunnels and other facilities near [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), as well as trackage over [Jamaica Bay](/wiki/Jamaica_Bay "Jamaica Bay"). The immediate damage was fixed within six months, but long-term resiliency and rehabilitation projects continued for several years. The recovery projects after the hurricane included the restoration of the new [South Ferry](/wiki/South_Ferry_(IRT_Broadway-Seventh_Avenue_Line) "South Ferry (IRT Broadway-Seventh Avenue Line)") station from 2012 to 2017; the full closure of the [Montague Street Tunnel](/wiki/Montague_Street_Tunnel "Montague Street Tunnel") from 2013 to 2014; and the partial [14th Street Tunnel shutdown](/wiki/14th_Street_Tunnel_shutdown "14th Street Tunnel shutdown") from 2019 to 2020.[[75]](#cite_note-81) Annual ridership on the New York City Subway system, which totaled nearly 1.7 billion in 2019, declined dramatically during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") and did not surpass one billion again until 2022.[[76]](#cite_note-Brachfeld_2022_k163-82)

### Construction methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Construction methods")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/7Line_2208_%289124955331%29.jpg/250px-7Line_2208_%289124955331%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:7Line_2208_(9124955331).jpg)

A stretch of subway track on the [7 Subway Extension](/wiki/7_Subway_Extension "7 Subway Extension")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/R-62A_Shuttle_Train_%40_Times_Square-42nd_Street_during_reconstruction.jpg/250px-R-62A_Shuttle_Train_%40_Times_Square-42nd_Street_during_reconstruction.jpg)](/wiki/File:R-62A_Shuttle_Train_@_Times_Square-42nd_Street_during_reconstruction.jpg)

R62A [42nd Street Shuttle](/wiki/42nd_Street_Shuttle "42nd Street Shuttle") train at [Times Square–42nd Street](/wiki/Times_Square%E2%80%9342nd_Street_station "Times Square–42nd Street station") during reconstruction

When the [IRT subway](/wiki/Early_history_of_the_IRT_subway "Early history of the IRT subway") debuted in 1904,[[41]](#cite_note-Walker1918-47) the typical tunnel construction method was [cut-and-cover](/wiki/Cut-and-cover "Cut-and-cover").[[77]](#cite_note-Construction-83) The street was torn up to dig the tunnel below before being rebuilt from above.[[77]](#cite_note-Construction-83) Traffic on the street above would be interrupted due to the digging up of the street.[[78]](#cite_note-84) Temporary steel and wooden bridges carried surface traffic above the construction.[[79]](#cite_note-85)

Contractors in this type of construction faced many obstacles, both natural and human-made. They had to deal with rock formations and groundwater, which required pumps. Twelve miles of sewers, as well as water and gas mains, electric conduits, and steam pipes had to be rerouted. Street railways had to be torn up to allow the work. The foundations of tall buildings often ran near the subway construction, and in some cases needed underpinning to ensure stability.[[80]](#cite_note-86)

This method worked well for digging soft dirt and gravel near the street surface.[[77]](#cite_note-Construction-83) [Tunnelling shields](/wiki/Tunnelling_shield "Tunnelling shield") were required for deeper sections, such as the Harlem and [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") tunnels, which used cast-iron tubes. Rock or concrete-lined tunnels were used on segments from 33rd to 42nd streets under [Park Avenue](/wiki/Park_Avenue_(Manhattan) "Park Avenue (Manhattan)"); 116th to 120th Streets under [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)"); 145th to [Dyckman Streets](/wiki/Dyckman_Street "Dyckman Street") (Fort George) under Broadway and [St. Nicholas Avenue](/wiki/St._Nicholas_Avenue "St. Nicholas Avenue"); and 96th Street and Broadway to Central Park North and [Lenox Avenue](/wiki/Lenox_Avenue "Lenox Avenue").[[77]](#cite_note-Construction-83)

About 40% of the subway system runs on surface or elevated tracks, including steel or cast-iron [elevated structures](/wiki/Elevated_railroad "Elevated railroad"), concrete [viaducts](/wiki/Viaduct "Viaduct"), [embankments](/wiki/Embankment_(transportation) "Embankment (transportation)"), [open cuts](/wiki/Cut_(earthmoving) "Cut (earthmoving)") and surface routes.[[81]](#cite_note-87) As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), there are 168 miles (270 km) of elevated tracks.[[82]](#cite_note-88) All of these construction methods are completely [grade-separated](/wiki/Grade-separated "Grade-separated") from road and pedestrian crossings, and most crossings of two subway tracks are grade-separated with [flying junctions](/wiki/Flying_junction "Flying junction"). The sole exceptions of at-grade junctions of two lines in regular service are the [142nd Street](/wiki/IRT_Lenox_Avenue_Line "IRT Lenox Avenue Line")[[83]](#cite_note-89) and [Myrtle Avenue](/wiki/Myrtle_Avenue_(BMT_Jamaica_Line) "Myrtle Avenue (BMT Jamaica Line)") junctions, whose tracks intersect at the same level, as well as the same-direction pairs of tracks on the [IRT Eastern Parkway Line](/wiki/IRT_Eastern_Parkway_Line "IRT Eastern Parkway Line") at [Rogers Junction](/wiki/Rogers_Junction "Rogers Junction").[[84]](#cite_note-90)[[85]](#cite_note-tracks-91)

The 7,700 workers who built the original subway lines were mostly [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") living in Manhattan.[[31]](#cite_note-PBS-37)[[86]](#cite_note-92)

More recent projects use [tunnel boring machines](/wiki/Tunnel_boring_machine "Tunnel boring machine"), which increase the cost. However, they minimize disruption at street level and avoid already existing utilities.[[87]](#cite_note-93) Examples of such projects include the [extension of the IRT Flushing Line](/wiki/7_Subway_Extension "7 Subway Extension")[[88]](#cite_note-94)[[89]](#cite_note-95)[[90]](#cite_note-96)[[91]](#cite_note-97) and the [IND Second Avenue Line](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway").[[92]](#cite_note-98)[[93]](#cite_note-99)[[94]](#cite_note-100)

### Expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Expansion")]

Main article: [Proposed expansion of the New York City Subway](/wiki/Proposed_expansion_of_the_New_York_City_Subway "Proposed expansion of the New York City Subway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Second_Avenue_Subway_Community_Information_Center_vc.jpg/250px-Second_Avenue_Subway_Community_Information_Center_vc.jpg)](/wiki/File:Second_Avenue_Subway_Community_Information_Center_vc.jpg)

Second Avenue Subway Community Information Center

Since the [opening](/wiki/Early_history_of_the_IRT_subway "Early history of the IRT subway") of the original New York City Subway line in 1904,[[41]](#cite_note-Walker1918-47) multiple official and planning agencies have proposed numerous extensions to the subway system. One of the more expansive proposals was the "[IND](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System") Second System", part of a plan to construct new subway lines in addition to taking over existing subway lines and railroad rights-of-way. The most grandiose IND Second Subway plan, conceived in 1929, was to be part of the city-operated IND, and was to comprise almost 1⁄3 of the current subway system.[[95]](#cite_note-NYTimes-OurGreatSubway-IND2ndSystem-1929-101)[[96]](#cite_note-1929-SubwayPlan-102) By 1939, with unification planned, all three systems were included within the plan, which was ultimately never carried out.[[97]](#cite_note-103)[[98]](#cite_note-nycsubway2-104) Many different plans were proposed over the years of the subway's existence, but expansion of the subway system mostly stopped during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[99]](#cite_note-105)

Though most of the routes proposed over the decades have never seen construction, discussion remains strong to develop some of these lines, to alleviate existing subway capacity constraints and overcrowding, the most notable being the [proposals for the Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Unbuilt_plans_for_the_Second_Avenue_Subway "Unbuilt plans for the Second Avenue Subway"). Plans for new lines date back to the early 1910s, and expansion plans have been proposed during many years of the system's existence.[[53]](#cite_note-Raskin-RoutesNotTaken-20132-59)[[95]](#cite_note-NYTimes-OurGreatSubway-IND2ndSystem-1929-101)

After the [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line") was completed in 1940,[[100]](#cite_note-SixthAvenueOpening-106) the city went into [great debt](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1946%E2%80%9377) "History of New York City (1946–77)"), and only 33 new stations have been added to the system since, nineteen of which were part of defunct railways that already existed. Five stations were on the abandoned [New York, Westchester and Boston Railway](/wiki/New_York,_Westchester_and_Boston_Railway "New York, Westchester and Boston Railway"), which was incorporated into the system in 1941 as the [IRT Dyre Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Dyre_Avenue_Line "IRT Dyre Avenue Line").[[101]](#cite_note-107) Fourteen more stations were on the abandoned LIRR [Rockaway Beach Branch](/wiki/Rockaway_Beach_Branch "Rockaway Beach Branch") (now the [IND Rockaway Line](/wiki/IND_Rockaway_Line "IND Rockaway Line")), which opened in 1955.[[102]](#cite_note-NYTimes-INDRkwyOpen-1956-108) Two stations ([57th Street](/wiki/57th_Street_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "57th Street (IND Sixth Avenue Line)") and [Grand Street](/wiki/Grand_Street_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "Grand Street (IND Sixth Avenue Line)")) were part of the [Chrystie Street Connection](/wiki/Chrystie_Street_Connection "Chrystie Street Connection"), and opened in 1968;[[103]](#cite_note-109)[[104]](#cite_note-110) the [Harlem–148th Street](/wiki/Harlem%E2%80%93148th_Street_(IRT_Lenox_Avenue_Line) "Harlem–148th Street (IRT Lenox Avenue Line)") terminal opened that same year in an unrelated project.[[105]](#cite_note-NYTImes-148LenoxOpen-1968-111) Six more new stations were built as part of a [1968 plan](/wiki/Program_for_Action "Program for Action"): three on the [Archer Avenue Lines](/wiki/Archer_Avenue_Lines "Archer Avenue Lines"), opened in 1988,[[68]](#cite_note-nytimes2-74) and three on the [63rd Street Lines](/wiki/63rd_Street_Lines "63rd Street Lines"), opened in 1989.[[67]](#cite_note-Lorch-73) The new [South Ferry](/wiki/South_Ferry_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "South Ferry (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") station was built and connected to the existing [Whitehall Street–South Ferry](/wiki/Whitehall_Street%E2%80%93South_Ferry_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "Whitehall Street–South Ferry (BMT Broadway Line)") station in 2009.[[106]](#cite_note-112) The one-stop [7 Subway Extension](/wiki/7_Subway_Extension "7 Subway Extension") to the [west side](/wiki/West_Side_(Manhattan) "West Side (Manhattan)") of Manhattan, consisting of the [34th Street–Hudson Yards](/wiki/34th_Street%E2%80%93Hudson_Yards_(IRT_Flushing_Line) "34th Street–Hudson Yards (IRT Flushing Line)") station, was opened in 2015,[[107]](#cite_note-113)[[108]](#cite_note-mta.info_2015-114)[[note 7]](#cite_note-115) and three stations on the [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway") on the [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side") were opened as part of [Phase 1 of the line](/wiki/Construction_of_the_Second_Avenue_Subway#Phase_1 "Construction of the Second Avenue Subway") at the beginning of 2017.[[109]](#cite_note-116)

A digital sign on the side of a [Bombardier R142](/wiki/R142_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R142 (New York City Subway car)") train on the [5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/MTA125.JPG/250px-MTA125.JPG)](/wiki/File:MTA125.JPG)

The [125th Street](/wiki/125th_Street_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "125th Street (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") station on the [IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line "IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line") in 2007

Many rapid transit systems run relatively static routings, so that a train "line" is more or less synonymous with a train "route". In New York City, routings change often, for various reasons. Within the [nomenclature of the subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_nomenclature "New York City Subway nomenclature"), the "line" describes the physical railroad track or series of tracks that a train "route" uses on its way from one terminal to another. "Routes" (also called "services") are distinguished by a letter or a number and "lines" have names. Trains display their route designation.[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23)

There are [28 train services](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_services "List of New York City Subway services") in the subway system, including [three short shuttles](/wiki/S_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "S (New York City Subway service)"). Each route has a color and a local or express designation representing the Manhattan trunk line of the service.[[118]](#cite_note-125)[[119]](#cite_note-Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority2019-126) New York City residents seldom refer to services by color (e.g., "blue line" or "green line") but out-of-towners and tourists often do.[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23)[[120]](#cite_note-127)[[121]](#cite_note-128)

The [1](/wiki/1_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "1 (New York City Subway service)"), [C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)"), [G](/wiki/G_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "G (New York City Subway service)"), [L](/wiki/L_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "L (New York City Subway service)"), [M](/wiki/M_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "M (New York City Subway service)"), [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)"), and [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") trains are fully local and make all stops. The [2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)"), [3](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)"), [4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)"), [5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)"), [A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)"), [B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)"), [D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)"), [E](/wiki/E_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "E (New York City Subway service)"), [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)"), [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), and [Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") trains have portions of [express](/wiki/Express_train "Express train") and local service. [J](/wiki/J_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "J (New York City Subway service)"), [Z](/wiki/Z_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Z (New York City Subway service)"), [6](/wiki/6_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6 (New York City Subway service)"), and [7](/wiki/7_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7 (New York City Subway service)") trains vary by direction, day, or time of day. The letter [S](/wiki/S_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "S (New York City Subway service)") is used for three shuttle services: [Franklin Avenue Shuttle](/wiki/Franklin_Avenue_Shuttle "Franklin Avenue Shuttle"), [Rockaway Park Shuttle](/wiki/Rockaway_Park_Shuttle "Rockaway Park Shuttle"), and [42nd Street Shuttle](/wiki/42nd_Street_Shuttle "42nd Street Shuttle").[[119]](#cite_note-Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority2019-126)[[122]](#cite_note-NYCS_const|map-129)

Though the subway system [operates on a 24-hour basis](/wiki/24/7 "24/7"),[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23) during late night hours some of the designated routes do not run, run as a shorter route (often referred to as the "shuttle train" version of its full-length counterpart) or run with a different stopping pattern. These are usually indicated by smaller, secondary route signage on station platforms.[[119]](#cite_note-Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority2019-126)[[123]](#cite_note-130) Because there is no nightly system shutdown for maintenance, tracks and stations must be maintained while the system is operating. This work sometimes necessitates service changes during midday, overnight hours, and weekends.[[124]](#cite_note-Haberman_2008-131)[[125]](#cite_note-Tangel-132)[[note 8]](#cite_note-133)

When parts of lines are temporarily shut down for construction purposes, the transit authority can substitute free [shuttle buses](/wiki/Shuttle_bus_service "Shuttle bus service") (using [MTA Regional Bus Operations bus fleet](/wiki/MTA_Regional_Bus_Operations_bus_fleet "MTA Regional Bus Operations bus fleet")) to replace the routes that would normally run on these lines.[[126]](#cite_note-134) The Transit Authority announces planned service changes through its website,[[127]](#cite_note-135) via placards that are posted on station and interior subway-car walls,[[128]](#cite_note-136) and through its [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") page.[[129]](#cite_note-137)

### Subway map

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Subway map")]

Main article: [New York City Subway map](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_map "New York City Subway map")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Official_New_York_City_Subway_Map_2013_vc.jpg/500px-Official_New_York_City_Subway_Map_2013_vc.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_New_York_City_Subway_Map_2013_vc.jpg)

The official New York City Subway map from June 2013. This is not the current map.

Since 2025, the [official transit maps of the New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_map "New York City Subway map") have been based on a 1972 design by [Massimo Vignelli](/wiki/Massimo_Vignelli "Massimo Vignelli").[[133]](#cite_note-141) Subway routes are grouped into colors and are arranged into horizontal, vertical, and diagonal strokes.[[134]](#cite_note-Hernandez_f085-142)[[135]](#cite_note-Ginsburg_q217-143) The map uses a white background, black dots for stations, and horizontal labels.[[136]](#cite_note-Russo-Lennon_l471-144)

Earlier diagrams of the subway, the first being produced in 1958, had the perception of being more geographically inaccurate than the diagrams today. The first iteration of the subway map by Vignelli, published by the MTA between 1972 and 1979, has become a modern classic but the MTA deemed the map flawed due to its placement of geographical elements.[[137]](#cite_note-145)[[138]](#cite_note-Hertz-Gothamist-146) In 1979, the design was replaced by one from [Michael Hertz Associates](/wiki/Michael_Hertz_Associates "Michael Hertz Associates"),[[139]](#cite_note-147) which was used until 2025.[[134]](#cite_note-Hernandez_f085-142)[[135]](#cite_note-Ginsburg_q217-143) The Hertz maps are not geographically accurate due to the complexity of the system (Manhattan being the smallest borough, but having the most services), but they do show major city streets as an aid to navigation. The newest edition took effect on June 27, 2010, and makes Manhattan bigger and Staten Island smaller, with minor tweaks happening to the map when more permanent changes occur.[[122]](#cite_note-NYCS_const|map-129)[[140]](#cite_note-148)

A late night-only version of the map was introduced on January 30, 2012.[[141]](#cite_note-149) On September 16, 2011, the MTA introduced a Vignelli-style interactive subway map, "The Weekender",[[142]](#cite_note-150) an [online map](/wiki/Online_map "Online map") that provides information about any planned work, from late Friday night to early Monday morning.[[143]](#cite_note-151)[[144]](#cite_note-152) In October 2020, the MTA launched a digital version of the map showing real-time service patterns and service changes, designed by [Work & Co](/wiki/Work_%26_Co "Work & Co").[[145]](#cite_note-6sqft_2020-153)[[146]](#cite_note-Bonanos_2020-154)

Several privately produced schematics are available online or in printed form, such as those by [Hagstrom Map](/wiki/Hagstrom_Map "Hagstrom Map").[[147]](#cite_note-155)

## Stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Stations")]

Main article: [New York City Subway stations](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_stations "New York City Subway stations")

See also: [List of New York City Subway stations](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_stations "List of New York City Subway stations")

[R62A](/wiki/R62A "R62A") [7](/wiki/7_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7 (New York City Subway service)") train arriving at [Vernon Boulevard–Jackson Avenue](/wiki/Vernon_Boulevard%E2%80%93Jackson_Avenue_station "Vernon Boulevard–Jackson Avenue station") station (43s)

Out of the [472 stations](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_stations#List_of_stations "New York City Subway stations"), 470 are served 24 hours a day.[[note 9]](#cite_note-156) Underground stations in the New York City Subway are typically accessed by staircases going down from street level. Many of these staircases are painted in a common shade of green, with slight or significant variations in design.[[148]](#cite_note-FNY-SubwayEntranceStylings-157) Other stations have unique entrances reflective of their location or date of construction. Several station entrance stairs, for example, are built into adjacent buildings.[[148]](#cite_note-FNY-SubwayEntranceStylings-157) Nearly all station entrances feature [color-coded globe or square lamps](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_stations#Lamps "New York City Subway stations") signifying their status as an entrance.[[149]](#cite_note-nytimes_20020813-158) The current number of stations is smaller than the peak of the system. In addition to the demolition of former elevated lines, which collectively have resulted in the demolition of over a hundred stations, other [closed stations](/wiki/List_of_closed_New_York_City_Subway_stations "List of closed New York City Subway stations") and unused portions of existing stations remain in parts of the system.[[150]](#cite_note-159)

[![The mezzanine at West Fourth Street station](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/West_4_St_mezzanine_vc.jpg/250px-West_4_St_mezzanine_vc.jpg)](/wiki/File:West_4_St_mezzanine_vc.jpg)

The long and wide mezzanine in the [West Fourth Street](/wiki/West_Fourth_Street%E2%80%93Washington_Square_(New_York_City_Subway) "West Fourth Street–Washington Square (New York City Subway)") station in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village")

### Concourse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Concourse")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Times_Square-42nd_Street_Entrance.JPG/250px-Times_Square-42nd_Street_Entrance.JPG)](/wiki/File:Times_Square-42nd_Street_Entrance.JPG)

An entrance to the [Times Square–42nd Street](/wiki/Times_Square%E2%80%9342nd_Street_station "Times Square–42nd Street station") and [Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/42nd_Street%E2%80%93Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "42nd Street–Port Authority Bus Terminal") stations

Many stations in the subway system have [mezzanines](/wiki/Mezzanine_(architecture) "Mezzanine (architecture)").[[151]](#cite_note-PCAC-ClosedSubwayEntrance-2001-160) Mezzanines allow for passengers to enter from multiple locations at an intersection and proceed to the correct platform without having to cross the street before entering. Inside mezzanines are [fare control](/wiki/Paid_area "Paid area") areas, where passengers physically pay their fare to enter the subway system.[[151]](#cite_note-PCAC-ClosedSubwayEntrance-2001-160)[[152]](#cite_note-MTA-JayLawrenceMetroTech-161) In many older stations, the fare control area is at platform level with no mezzanine crossovers.[[153]](#cite_note-162) Many [side platformed](/wiki/Side_platform "Side platform") elevated stations also have platform-level fare control with no common station house between directions of service.[[37]](#cite_note-NYCSorg-BMTJamaica-43)

Upon entering a station, passengers may use station booths (formerly known as token booths)[[154]](#cite_note-163) or vending machines to buy their fare, which is currently stored in a [MetroCard](/wiki/MetroCard "MetroCard") or [OMNY](/wiki/OMNY "OMNY") card. Each station has at least one booth, typically located at the busiest entrance.[[155]](#cite_note-164) After swiping the card at a turnstile, customers enter the fare-controlled area of the station and continue to the platforms.[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23) Inside fare control are "Off-Hours Waiting Areas", which consist of benches and are identified by a yellow sign.[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23)[[156]](#cite_note-MTA-RidingSafely-165)[[157]](#cite_note-166)

Only five stations (Eastchester–Dyre Avenue, Baychester Avenue, Gun Hill Road, Pelham Parkway and Morris Park) of the whole NYC subway do not have at least a booth that is staffed 24 hours per day, 7 days per week.[[158]](#cite_note-167)

### Platforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Platforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/59th_Street%E2%80%93Columbus_Circle_%28New_York_City_Subway%29_by_David_Shankbone.jpg/250px-59th_Street%E2%80%93Columbus_Circle_%28New_York_City_Subway%29_by_David_Shankbone.jpg)](/wiki/File:59th_Street%E2%80%93Columbus_Circle_(New_York_City_Subway)_by_David_Shankbone.jpg)

The [IND Eighth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line "IND Eighth Avenue Line") station at [59th Street–Columbus Circle](/wiki/59th_Street%E2%80%93Columbus_Circle_station "59th Street–Columbus Circle station")

A typical subway station has waiting platforms ranging from 480 to 600 feet (150 to 180 m) long. Some are longer.[[49]](#cite_note-Feinman2000-55)[[159]](#cite_note-168) Platforms of former commuter rail stations—such as those on the [IND Rockaway Line](/wiki/IND_Rockaway_Line "IND Rockaway Line"), are even longer. With the many different lines in the system, one platform often serves more than one service. Passengers need to look at the overhead signs to see which trains stop there and when, and at the arriving train to identify it.[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23)

There are several common platform configurations. On a double track line, a station may have one [center island platform](/wiki/Island_platform "Island platform") used for trains in both directions, or two [side platforms](/wiki/Side_platform "Side platform"), one for each direction. For lines with three or four tracks with express service, local stops will have side platforms and the middle one or two tracks will not stop at the station. On these lines, express stations typically have two island platforms, one for each direction. Each island platform provides a [cross-platform interchange](/wiki/Cross-platform_interchange "Cross-platform interchange") between local and express services. Some four-track lines with express service have two tracks each on two levels and use both island and side platforms.[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23)

### Accessibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Accessibility")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/14-6_elevator.jpg/250px-14-6_elevator.jpg)](/wiki/File:14-6_elevator.jpg)

Street elevator serving as one of three accessible entrances to the [14th Street/6th Avenue](/wiki/14th_Street/Sixth_Avenue_station "14th Street/Sixth Avenue station") station

Main article: [Accessibility of the Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Accessibility_of_the_Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Accessibility of the Metropolitan Transportation Authority")

Since the majority of the system was built before 1990, the year the [Americans with Disabilities Act](/wiki/Americans_with_Disabilities_Act_of_1990 "Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990") (ADA) went into effect, many New York City Subway stations were not designed to be accessible to all.[[160]](#cite_note-169) Since then, elevators have been built in newly constructed stations to comply with the ADA. (Most grade-level stations required little modification to meet ADA standards.) Many accessible stations have [AutoGate](/wiki/MetroCard_(New_York_City)#Disabled/Senior_Citizen_Reduced-Fare_MetroCard "MetroCard (New York City)") access.[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23)[[161]](#cite_note-170) In addition, the MTA identified "key stations", high-traffic and/or geographically important stations, which must conform to the ADA when they are extensively renovated. Under plans from the MTA in 2016, the number of ADA accessible stations would go up to 144 by 2020.[[162]](#cite_note-171) As of January 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), there were 159 ADA-accessible stations.[[163]](#cite_note-172) There are also 284 escalators in the subway system as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit).[[164]](#cite_note-173)[[165]](#cite_note-174)

Over the years, the MTA has been involved in a number of lawsuits over the lack of accessibility in its stations. The Eastern Paralyzed Veterans Association filed what may have been the first of these suits in 1979, based on state law.[[166]](#cite_note-175) The lawsuits have relied on a number of different legal bases, but most have centered around the MTA's failure to include accessibility as a part of its plans for remodeling various stations.[[167]](#cite_note-Rosenberg2017-176) As of January 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), ADA-accessibility projects are expected to be started or completed at 51 stations as part of the 2020–2024 Capital Program.[[168]](#cite_note-MassapequaPark-177)[[169]](#cite_note-178) This would allow one of every two to four stations on every line to be accessible, so that all non-accessible stops would be a maximum of two stops from an accessible station.[[170]](#cite_note-fastforward-179): 39

In 2022, the MTA agreed in a settlement to make 95 percent of subway and Staten Island Railway stations accessible by 2055.[[171]](#cite_note-nyt-2022-06-222-180) By comparison, all but one of Boston's [MBTA subway](/wiki/MBTA_subway "MBTA subway") stations are accessible, the [Chicago "L"](/wiki/Chicago_%22L%22 "Chicago \"L\"") plans all stations to be accessible in the 2030s,[[172]](#cite_note-181) the [Toronto subway](/wiki/Toronto_subway "Toronto subway") will be fully accessible by 2025,[[173]](#cite_note-TTC-2021-06-23-182) and [Montreal Metro](/wiki/Montreal_Metro "Montreal Metro") plans all stations to be accessible by 2038.[[174]](#cite_note-183) Both the Boston and Chicago systems are as old or older than the New York City Subway, though each of these systems has fewer stations than the New York City Subway.[[175]](#cite_note-NYDN-GroupsSueMTA-2016-184)[[176]](#cite_note-Office_of_the_New_York_City_Comptroller_Scott_M._Stringer_2018-185) Newer systems, such as the [Washington Metro](/wiki/Washington_Metro "Washington Metro") and [Bay Area Rapid Transit](/wiki/Bay_Area_Rapid_Transit "Bay Area Rapid Transit"), have been fully accessible from their opening in the 1970s.[[177]](#cite_note-Chang_2020_s188-186)

## Rolling stock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rolling stock")]
