Main article: [New York City Subway rolling stock](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_rolling_stock "New York City Subway rolling stock")

In November 2016, the New York City Subway had 6712 cars on the roster.[[note 10]](#cite_note-187)
A typical New York City Subway train consists of 8 to 11 cars, although shuttles can have as few as two, and the train can range from 150 to 600 feet (46 to 183 m) in length.[[178]](#cite_note-188) The system maintains two separate fleets of cars, one for the A Division routes and another for the B Division routes.[[179]](#cite_note-www.thejoekorner.com-189) A Division equipment is approximately 8 feet 9 inches (2.67 m) wide and 51 feet 4 inches (15.65 m) long, whereas B Division equipment is about 10 feet (3.05 m) wide and either 60 feet 6 inches (18.44 m) or 75 feet (22.86 m) long.[[180]](#cite_note-190) 75-foot cars cannot be used over the [BMT Eastern Division](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway_nomenclature#BMT "New York City Subway nomenclature") in regular service due to tight turning radii on Eastern Division lines;[[181]](#cite_note-DCP-Broadway-Junction-Pt3-191)[[182]](#cite_note-192) the turning radii for curves on Eastern Division lines are as tight as 175 feet (53 m).[[181]](#cite_note-DCP-Broadway-Junction-Pt3-191) Each train typically has two staff members on board: an operator at the front, who drives the train, and a conductor at the center, who makes announcements, assists operators, and is responsible for passenger safety and service.[[183]](#cite_note-193)

Cars purchased by the City of New York since the inception of the IND and the other divisions beginning in 1948 are identified by the letter "R" followed by a number; e.g.: [R32](/wiki/R32_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R32 (New York City Subway car)").[[179]](#cite_note-www.thejoekorner.com-189) This number is the contract number under which the cars were purchased.[[184]](#cite_note-joekorner-194) Cars with nearby contract numbers (e.g.: [R1](/wiki/R1_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R1 (New York City Subway car)") through [R9](/wiki/R9_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R9 (New York City Subway car)"), or [R26](/wiki/R26_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R26 (New York City Subway car)") through [R29](/wiki/R29_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R29 (New York City Subway car)"), or [R143](/wiki/R143 "R143") through [R179](/wiki/R179 "R179")) may be relatively identical, despite being purchased under different contracts and possibly built by different manufacturers.[[185]](#cite_note-NYTimes-NYCSPromiseInfo-Nov2005-195)

From 2000 to 2024, the [R142](/wiki/R142_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R142 (New York City Subway car)"), [R142A](/wiki/R142A_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R142A (New York City Subway car)"), [R143](/wiki/R143_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R143 (New York City Subway car)"), [R160](/wiki/R160_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R160 (New York City Subway car)"), [R179](/wiki/R179_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R179 (New York City Subway car)"), [R188](/wiki/R188_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R188 (New York City Subway car)") and [R211](/wiki/R211_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R211 (New York City Subway car)") were placed into service.[[186]](#cite_note-196)[[187]](#cite_note-MTA-R160-NTT-E-Dec2008-197) These cars are collectively known as [New Technology Trains](/wiki/New_Technology_Train "New Technology Train") (NTTs) due to modern innovations such as information screens, recorded train announcements, and the ability to facilitate [Communication-Based Train Control (CBTC)](/wiki/Communication-based_train_control "Communication-based train control").[[187]](#cite_note-MTA-R160-NTT-E-Dec2008-197)[[188]](#cite_note-NYPress-NYCSNTTVoice-2003-198)

As part of the 2017–2020 MTA Financial Plan, 600 subway cars will have electronic display signs installed to improve customer experience.[[189]](#cite_note-mta.info2016-199)

## Fares

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Fares")]

Main article: [New York City transit fares](/wiki/New_York_City_transit_fares "New York City transit fares")

Riders pay a single fare to enter the subway system and may transfer between trains at no extra cost until they exit via station turnstiles; the fare is a flat rate regardless of how far or how long the rider travels. Thus, riders must tap a contactless payment card or smartphone on an [OMNY](/wiki/OMNY "OMNY") reader (or swipe a [MetroCard](/wiki/MetroCard "MetroCard") with remaining balance[[190]](#cite_note-Martinez_202512-200)[[191]](#cite_note-Buchwald_202512-201)) upon entering the subway system, but not a second time upon leaving.[[192]](#cite_note-web.mta.info-202)

As of December 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), nearly all fares are paid by OMNY,[[193]](#cite_note-203) and as of January 4, 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), the base fare is $3.[[194]](#cite_note-204)[[195]](#cite_note-205) Reduced fares are available for the elderly and people with disabilities.[[17]](#cite_note-MTA-HowtoRideSubway-23)[[196]](#cite_note-206) In addition, there is a weekly [fare cap](/wiki/Fare_capping "Fare capping") for subways and buses; after passengers pay 12 fares with the same fare-payment medium in a 7-day period, any additional trips during the same period are free.[[197]](#cite_note-207)

Fares were stored in a money room at [370 Jay Street](/wiki/370_Jay_Street "370 Jay Street") in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") starting in 1951, when the building opened as a headquarters for the [New York City Board of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_City_Board_of_Transportation "New York City Board of Transportation").[[198]](#cite_note-208) The building is close to the lines of all three subway divisions (the IRT, BMT, and IND) and thus was a convenient location to collect fares, including tokens and cash, via [money trains](/wiki/Money_train "Money train"). Passageways from the subway stations, including a visible door in the Jay Street IND station, lead to a money sorting room in the basement of the building.[[199]](#cite_note-utc20160212-209)[[200]](#cite_note-Atlas-370JaySt-Nov2015-210) The money trains were replaced by [armored trucks](/wiki/Armored_truck "Armored truck") in 2006.[[199]](#cite_note-utc20160212-209)[[200]](#cite_note-Atlas-370JaySt-Nov2015-210)[[201]](#cite_note-211)

### MetroCard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: MetroCard")]

Main article: [MetroCard](/wiki/MetroCard "MetroCard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/8b/MetroCard.SVG/250px-MetroCard.SVG.png)](/wiki/File:MetroCard.SVG)

The current MetroCard design

In June 1993, a fare system called the [MetroCard](/wiki/MetroCard "MetroCard") was introduced, which allows riders to use [magnetic stripe cards](/wiki/Magnetic_stripe_card "Magnetic stripe card") that store the value equal to the amount paid to a subway station booth clerk or vending machine.[[202]](#cite_note-212) The MetroCard was enhanced in 1997 to allow passengers to make free transfers between subways and buses within two hours and several MetroCard-only [transfers between subway stations](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_transfer_stations "List of New York City Subway transfer stations") were added in 2001.[[203]](#cite_note-213)[[204]](#cite_note-214) With the addition of unlimited-ride MetroCards in 1998, the New York City Transit system was the last major transit system in the United States with the exception of [BART](/wiki/BART "BART") in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") to introduce passes for unlimited bus and rapid transit travel.[[205]](#cite_note-215) MetroCard sales ended December 31, 2025,[[206]](#cite_note-216)[[207]](#cite_note-217) although existing MetroCards could continue to be used until their balance was depleted or the card expired.[[190]](#cite_note-Martinez_202512-200)[[191]](#cite_note-Buchwald_202512-201)

### OMNY

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: OMNY")]

Main article: [OMNY](/wiki/OMNY "OMNY")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/OMNY_reader_in_NYC_subway.jpg/250px-OMNY_reader_in_NYC_subway.jpg)](/wiki/File:OMNY_reader_in_NYC_subway.jpg)

OMNY replaced the MetroCard.

On October 23, 2017, it was announced that the MetroCard would be phased out and replaced by [OMNY](/wiki/OMNY "OMNY"), a [contactless fare payment](/wiki/Contactless_payment "Contactless payment") system by [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego")-based [Cubic Transportation Systems](/wiki/Cubic_Transportation_Systems "Cubic Transportation Systems"), with fare payment being made using [Apple Pay](/wiki/Apple_Pay "Apple Pay"), [Google Pay](/wiki/Google_Pay_(payment_method) "Google Pay (payment method)"), debit/credit cards with [near-field communication](/wiki/Near-field_communication "Near-field communication") technology, or [radio-frequency identification](/wiki/Radio-frequency_identification "Radio-frequency identification") cards.[[208]](#cite_note-218)[[209]](#cite_note-:2a-219) OMNY was available on all MTA buses and at all subway stations by December 31, 2020.[[210]](#cite_note-cbs20201231-220)

## Modernization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Modernization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Reopening_of_53rd_St_ESI_Station_%2836710339210%29.jpg/250px-Reopening_of_53rd_St_ESI_Station_%2836710339210%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reopening_of_53rd_St_ESI_Station_(36710339210).jpg)

A subway station rebuilt under the Enhanced Station Initiative

Main article: [Technology of the New York City Subway](/wiki/Technology_of_the_New_York_City_Subway "Technology of the New York City Subway")

Since the late 20th century, the MTA has started several projects to maintain and improve the subway. In the 1990s, it started converting the [BMT Canarsie Line](/wiki/BMT_Canarsie_Line "BMT Canarsie Line") to use [communications-based train control](/wiki/Communications-based_train_control "Communications-based train control"), utilizing a [moving block](/wiki/Moving_block "Moving block") signal system that allowed more trains to use the tracks and thus increasing passenger capacity.[[211]](#cite_note-221) After the Canarsie Line tests were successful, the MTA expanded the automation program in the 2000s and 2010s to include other lines.[[212]](#cite_note-222)[[213]](#cite_note-223) As part of another program called FASTRACK, the MTA started closing sections of lines during weekday nights in 2012,
in order to allow workers to clean these lines without being hindered by train movements.[[214]](#cite_note-224) It expanded the program beyond Manhattan the next year after noticing how efficient the FASTRACK program was compared to previous service diversions.[[215]](#cite_note-225) In 2015, the MTA announced a wide-ranging improvement program as part of the 2015–2019 Capital Program. Thirty stations would be extensively rebuilt under the [Enhanced Station Initiative](/wiki/Enhanced_Station_Initiative "Enhanced Station Initiative"), and new [R211](/wiki/R211_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R211 (New York City Subway car)") subway cars would be able to fit more passengers.[[216]](#cite_note-226)[[217]](#cite_note-227)

The MTA has also started some projects to improve passenger amenities. It added train arrival "countdown clocks" to most [A Division](/wiki/A_Division_(New_York_City_Subway) "A Division (New York City Subway)") stations (except on the [IRT Flushing Line](/wiki/IRT_Flushing_Line "IRT Flushing Line"), serving the [7](/wiki/7_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7 (New York City Subway service)") and [<7>](/wiki/7d_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7d (New York City Subway service)")​ trains) and the [BMT Canarsie Line](/wiki/BMT_Canarsie_Line "BMT Canarsie Line") ([L](/wiki/L_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "L (New York City Subway service)") train) by late 2011, allowing passengers on these routes to see train arrival times using real-time data.[[218]](#cite_note-228) A similar countdown-clock project for the [B Division](/wiki/B_Division_(New_York_City_Subway) "B Division (New York City Subway)") and the Flushing Line was deferred[[219]](#cite_note-229) until 2016, when a new [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth")-based clock system was tested successfully.[[220]](#cite_note-230) Beginning in 2011, the MTA also started "Help Point" to aid with emergency calls or station agent assistance.[[221]](#cite_note-231) The Help Point project was deemed successful, and the MTA subsequently installed Help Points in all stations.[[222]](#cite_note-Smith_Insider_20161-232) Interactive [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") "On The Go! Travel Station" kiosks, which give station advisories, itineraries, and timetables, were installed starting in 2011,[[223]](#cite_note-233) with the program also being expanded after a successful pilot.[[224]](#cite_note-234) Cellular phone and wireless data in stations, first installed in 2011 as part of yet another pilot program,[[225]](#cite_note-235) was also expanded systemwide due to positive passenger feedback.[[222]](#cite_note-Smith_Insider_20161-232) Finally, credit-card trials at several subway stations in 2006 and 2010[[226]](#cite_note-236)[[227]](#cite_note-237) led to proposals for [contactless payment](/wiki/Contactless_payment "Contactless payment") to replace the aging MetroCard.[[228]](#cite_note-238)

## Safety and security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Safety and security")]

### Signaling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Signaling")]

Main article: [Signaling of the New York City Subway](/wiki/Signaling_of_the_New_York_City_Subway "Signaling of the New York City Subway")

Signaling has evolved during a century of operation, and MTA uses a mixture of old and new systems. Most routes use [block signaling](/wiki/Block_signaling "Block signaling") but a few routes are also being retrofitted with [communications-based train control](/wiki/Communications-based_train_control "Communications-based train control") (CBTC), which would allow trains to run without train operator input.[[229]](#cite_note-239)[[230]](#cite_note-rpa-signals-240)

#### Wayside block signaling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Wayside block signaling")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/34_St-Hudson_Yards_Station_%2821389427245%29.jpg/250px-34_St-Hudson_Yards_Station_%2821389427245%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:34_St-Hudson_Yards_Station_(21389427245).jpg)

Example of a wayside block signal at the [34th Street–Hudson Yards](/wiki/34th_Street%E2%80%93Hudson_Yards_(IRT_Flushing_Line) "34th Street–Hudson Yards (IRT Flushing Line)") station

The system currently uses [automatic block signaling](/wiki/Automatic_block_signaling "Automatic block signaling") with fixed wayside signals and automatic [train stops](/wiki/Train_stop "Train stop") to provide safe train operation across the whole system.[[230]](#cite_note-rpa-signals-240) The New York City Subway system has, for the most part, used block signaling since its first line opened, and many portions of the current signaling system were installed between the 1930s and 1960s. These signals work by preventing trains from entering a "block" occupied by another train. Typically, the blocks are 1,000 feet (300 m) long.[[231]](#cite_note-Somers2015-241) Red and green lights show whether a block is occupied or vacant. The train's maximum speed will depend on how many blocks are open in front of it. The signals do not register a train's speed, nor where in the block the train is located.[[232]](#cite_note-RPA-Moving_Forward-242)[[233]](#cite_note-243)

Subway trains are stopped mechanically at all signals showing "stop". To make [train stops](/wiki/Train_protection_system "Train protection system") safe and effective, wayside trippers must not be moved to trip ("stop") position until the train has fully passed.[[231]](#cite_note-Somers2015-241)[[234]](#cite_note-Trip_Stop-244)

#### Communications-based train control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Communications-based train control")]

In the late 1990s and early 2000s, the MTA began automating the subway by installing CBTC, which supplements rather than replaces the fixed-block signal system; it allows trains to operate more closely together with lower [headways](/wiki/Headway "Headway"). The [BMT Canarsie Line](/wiki/BMT_Canarsie_Line "BMT Canarsie Line"), on which the [L](/wiki/L_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "L (New York City Subway service)") train runs, was chosen for pilot testing because it is a self-contained line that does not operate in conjunction with other lines. CBTC became operational in February 2009.[[235]](#cite_note-245) Due to an unexpected ridership increase, the MTA ordered additional cars, and increased service from 15 trains to 26 trains per hour, an achievement beyond the capability of the block system.[[236]](#cite_note-neuman-May22-246) The total cost of the project was $340 million.[[231]](#cite_note-Somers2015-241)

After the success of the BMT Canarsie Line automation, the [IRT Flushing Line](/wiki/IRT_Flushing_Line "IRT Flushing Line"), carrying the [7](/wiki/7_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7 (New York City Subway service)") and [<7>](/wiki/7d_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7d (New York City Subway service)")​ trains, was next chosen to get CBTC.[[237]](#cite_note-247) Estimated to cost US$1.4 billion,[[238]](#cite_note-248) the project was completed in November 2018.[[239]](#cite_note-MTA-CPOC-Nov2018-249): 11–12 By 2018, CBTC was in the process of being installed on several other routes as well, particularly the [IND Queens Boulevard Line](/wiki/IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line "IND Queens Boulevard Line") ([E](/wiki/E_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "E (New York City Subway service)"), ​[F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)"), [<F>](/wiki/Fd_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Fd (New York City Subway service)"), ​[M](/wiki/M_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "M (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") trains) and [IND Culver Line](/wiki/IND_Culver_Line "IND Culver Line") ([F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)"), [<F>](/wiki/Fd_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Fd (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[G](/wiki/G_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "G (New York City Subway service)") trains).[[239]](#cite_note-MTA-CPOC-Nov2018-249) The total cost for the entire Queens Boulevard Line is estimated at over $900 million,[[240]](#cite_note-250) and the Queens Boulevard CBTC project was completed in 2022.[[189]](#cite_note-mta.info2016-199) Funding for CBTC on the [IND Eighth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line "IND Eighth Avenue Line") is also provided in the 2015–2019 capital plan,[[241]](#cite_note-251) and the [IND Crosstown Line](/wiki/IND_Crosstown_Line "IND Crosstown Line")[[242]](#cite_note-252) and [IND Fulton Street Line](/wiki/IND_Fulton_Street_Line "IND Fulton Street Line") were also being equipped with CBTC as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit).[[243]](#cite_note-253) The widespread installation of CBTC includes retrofitting many newer subway cars and replacement of older cars.[[244]](#cite_note-Daily_Intelligencer2016-254)

Eventually, the MTA has plans to automate a much larger portion, using [One Person Train Operation](/wiki/One_Person_Train_Operation "One Person Train Operation") (OPTO) in conjunction with CBTC. At the current pace of installation, it would take 175 years for CBTC to be installed at a cost of $20 billion.[[231]](#cite_note-Somers2015-241) The Flushing line operated at almost 30 trains an hour using the signal system installed when the line was built, but after the CBTC installation it became possible that an additional two trains per hour could be operated.[[245]](#cite_note-255) In March 2018, [New York City Transit Authority](/wiki/New_York_City_Transit_Authority "New York City Transit Authority") president [Andy Byford](/wiki/Andy_Byford "Andy Byford") announced a new plan for resignaling the subway with CBTC, which would only take 10 to 15 years, compared to the previous estimate of 40 years. This would cost $8 to $15 billion.[[246]](#cite_note-256)[[247]](#cite_note-257)

The New York City Subway uses a system known as Automatic Train Supervision (ATS) for dispatching and train routing on the [A Division](/wiki/A_Division_(New_York_City_Subway) "A Division (New York City Subway)")[[248]](#cite_note-ats-258) (the Flushing line and the trains used on the 7 and <7>​ services do not have ATS.)[[248]](#cite_note-ats-258) ATS allows dispatchers in the Operations Control Center (OCC) to see where trains are in real time, and whether each individual train is running early or late.[[248]](#cite_note-ats-258) Dispatchers can hold trains for connections, re-route trains, or short-turn trains to provide better service when a disruption causes delays.[[248]](#cite_note-ats-258)

### Train accidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Train accidents")]

Main article: [New York City Subway accidents](/wiki/History_of_the_New_York_City_Subway#Accidents "History of the New York City Subway")

Despite the signal system, there have been at least 64 major train accidents since 1918, when a train bound for [South Ferry](/wiki/South_Ferry_(IRT_elevated_station) "South Ferry (IRT elevated station)") collided with two trains halted near [Jackson Avenue](/wiki/Jackson_Avenue_(IRT_White_Plains_Road_Line) "Jackson Avenue (IRT White Plains Road Line)") on the [IRT White Plains Road Line](/wiki/IRT_White_Plains_Road_Line "IRT White Plains Road Line") in the Bronx.[[249]](#cite_note-Accidents-259) Several accidents resulted when the train operator ran through red signals and rear-ended the subway train in front of it; this resulted from the signaling practice of "keying by", which allowed train operators to bypass red signals. The deadliest accident, the [Malbone Street Wreck](/wiki/Malbone_Street_Wreck "Malbone Street Wreck"), occurred on November 1, 1918, beneath the intersection of [Flatbush Avenue](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue "Flatbush Avenue"), [Ocean Avenue](/wiki/Ocean_Avenue_(Brooklyn) "Ocean Avenue (Brooklyn)"), and Malbone Street (the latter of which is now Empire Boulevard) near the [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Prospect Park (BMT Brighton Line)") station of the then-BRT [Brighton Line](/wiki/BMT_Brighton_Line "BMT Brighton Line") in Brooklyn, killing 93 people.[[250]](#cite_note-260) As a result of accidents, especially more recent ones such as the [1995 Williamsburg Bridge crash](/wiki/1995_Williamsburg_Bridge_crash "1995 Williamsburg Bridge crash"), timer signals were installed. These signals have resulted in reduced speeds across the system. Accidents such as [derailments](/wiki/Derailment "Derailment") are also due to broken equipment, such as the rails and the train itself.[[249]](#cite_note-Accidents-259)

### Passenger safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Passenger safety")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/168th_Street_IRT_Broadway_2.JPG/250px-168th_Street_IRT_Broadway_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:168th_Street_IRT_Broadway_2.JPG)

Yellow platform edges, yellow staircase steps and yellow railings, painted for safety, at the IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line platform at 168th Street

#### Track safety and suicides

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Track safety and suicides")]

A portion of subway-related deaths in New York consists of [suicides](/wiki/Suicides "Suicides") committed by jumping in front of an oncoming train. Between 1990 and 2003, 343 subway-related suicides were registered out of a citywide total of 7,394 (4.6%) and subway-related suicides increased by 30%, despite a decline in overall suicide numbers.[[251]](#cite_note-261)

Due to increase in people hit by trains in 2013,[[252]](#cite_note-262) in late 2013 and early 2014 the MTA started a test program, with four systems installed and strategies instituted to eliminate the number of people hit by trains. [Closed-circuit television cameras](/wiki/Closed-circuit_television_camera "Closed-circuit television camera"), a web of [laser beams](/wiki/Laser_beam "Laser beam") stretched across the tracks, [radio frequencies](/wiki/Radio_frequency "Radio frequency") transmitted across the tracks, and [thermal imaging cameras](/wiki/Thermal_imaging_camera "Thermal imaging camera") focused on the station's tracks were installed.[[253]](#cite_note-thestreet-263) The tests were successful enough that the 2015–2019 capital program included similar installations system-wide.[[254]](#cite_note-264)

The MTA also expressed interest in starting a pilot program to install [platform edge doors](/wiki/Platform_edge_doors "Platform edge doors").[[255]](#cite_note-265) Several planned stations in the New York City Subway may possibly feature [platform screen doors](/wiki/Platform_screen_doors "Platform screen doors"), including at future stations such as those part of the [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway").[[256]](#cite_note-266) In October 2017, it was announced that as part of a [pilot program](/wiki/Pilot_experiment "Pilot experiment"), the [Third Avenue](/wiki/Third_Avenue_(BMT_Canarsie_Line) "Third Avenue (BMT Canarsie Line)") station would be refitted with platform screen doors during the [14th Street Tunnel shutdown](/wiki/14th_Street_Tunnel_shutdown "14th Street Tunnel shutdown") in 2019–2020.[[257]](#cite_note-267) The $30 million for the platform edge door pilot program was diverted to another project in 2018.[[258]](#cite_note-268) Following a series of incidents, MTA announced another PSD pilot program at three stations in February 2022: the [7](/wiki/7_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7 (New York City Subway service)") and [<7>](/wiki/7d_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7d (New York City Subway service)")​ trains' platform at [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square_(IRT_Flushing_Line) "Times Square (IRT Flushing Line)"); the [E](/wiki/E_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "E (New York City Subway service)") train's platform at [Sutphin Boulevard–Archer Avenue–JFK Airport](/wiki/Sutphin_Boulevard%E2%80%93Archer_Avenue%E2%80%93JFK_Airport_(IND_Archer_Avenue_Line) "Sutphin Boulevard–Archer Avenue–JFK Airport (IND Archer Avenue Line)"); and the Third Avenue station.[[259]](#cite_note-nyt-2022-02-23-269) Numerous challenges come with platform doors. Some subway lines operate multiple subway car models, and their doors do not align.[[260]](#cite_note-wsj.com-270) Many platforms are not strong enough to hold the additional weight of a platform barrier, thus requiring extensive renovations if they were to be installed.[[260]](#cite_note-wsj.com-270)

#### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Crime")]

Main article: [History of the New York City Subway § Crime](/wiki/History_of_the_New_York_City_Subway#Crime "History of the New York City Subway")

Crime rates have varied, but there was a downward trend from the 1990s to 2014.[[261]](#cite_note-271) To fight crime, various approaches have been used over the years, including an "If You See Something, Say Something" campaign[[262]](#cite_note-MTA.info_(YouTube)2011-272) and, starting in 2016, banning people who commit a crime in the subway system from entering the system for a certain length of time.[[263]](#cite_note-273)

In July 1985, the [Citizens Crime Commission of New York City](/wiki/Citizens_Crime_Commission_of_New_York_City "Citizens Crime Commission of New York City") published a study showing riders abandoning the subway, fearing the frequent robberies and generally bad circumstances.[[264]](#cite_note-City-274) Crime rates in the subway and the city dropped in 1993, part of a larger citywide decrease in crime.[[265]](#cite_note-275) [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg") stated in a November 2004 press release: "Today, the subway system is safer than it has been at any time since we started tabulating subway crime statistics nearly 40 years ago."[[266]](#cite_note-276) Although ridership decreased by 40 percent from 2019 to 2022, the number of crimes in the system remained roughly the same, prompting riders to express concerns over increased crime.[[267]](#cite_note-277)[[268]](#cite_note-NPR.org_2022-278) The subway recorded eight murders in 2021, the highest annual total in 25 years;[[269]](#cite_note-Guse_2022-279) by October 2022, nine people had been murdered that year alone.[[270]](#cite_note-280)[[271]](#cite_note-281)

The subway system has been the target of some mass attacks, though such attacks are relatively rare.[[272]](#cite_note-Chang_2022-282) On December 11, 2017, there was an attempted bombing at the [Times Square–42nd Street station](/wiki/Times_Square%E2%80%9342nd_Street_station "Times Square–42nd Street station"), injuring four people including the attacker.[[273]](#cite_note-283) On April 12, 2022, a [shooting attack](/wiki/2022_New_York_City_Subway_attack "2022 New York City Subway attack") occurred on the [N train](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), injuring 29 people including 10 who were shot.[[274]](#cite_note-Shapiro_Katersky_2022-284)

Since January 2025, two NYPD officers have patrolled each train between 9 p.m. and the following day at 5 a.m. The police move from car to car each time the train pulls into a station.[[275]](#cite_note-285)[[276]](#cite_note-286)

#### Photography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Photography")]

After the September 11, 2001, attacks, the MTA exercised extreme caution regarding anyone taking photographs or recording video inside the system and proposed banning all photography and recording in a meeting around June 2004.[[277]](#cite_note-287) Due to strong response from both the public and from civil rights groups, the rule of conduct was dropped. In November 2004, the MTA again put this rule up for approval, but was again denied,[[278]](#cite_note-288) though many police officers and transit workers still confront or harass people taking photographs or videos.[[279]](#cite_note-289) On April 3, 2009, the NYPD issued a directive to officers stating that it is legal to take pictures within the subway system so long as it is not accompanied with suspicious activity.[[280]](#cite_note-290)

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), the MTA Rules of Conduct, Restricted Areas and Activities section states that anyone may take pictures or record videos, provided that they do not use any of three tools: lights, reflectors, or tripods. These three tools are permitted only by members of the press who have identification issued by the NYPD.[[281]](#cite_note-291)

#### Terrorism prevention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Terrorism prevention")]

See also: [New York City Transit Police](/wiki/New_York_City_Transit_Police "New York City Transit Police")

On July 22, 2005, in response to [bombings in London](/wiki/July_2005_London_bombings "July 2005 London bombings"), the [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") introduced a new policy of randomly searching passengers' bags as they approached turnstiles. The NYPD claimed that no form of [racial profiling](/wiki/Racial_profiling "Racial profiling") would be conducted when these searches actually took place. The NYPD has come under fire from some groups that claim purely random searches without any form of [threat assessment](/wiki/Threat_assessment "Threat assessment") would be ineffectual. Donna Lieberman, executive director of the [NYCLU](/wiki/NYCLU "NYCLU"), stated, "This NYPD bag search policy is unprecedented, unlawful and ineffective. It is essential that police be aggressive in maintaining security in public transportation. But our very real concerns about terrorism do not justify the NYPD subjecting millions of innocent people to suspicionless searches in a way that does not identify any person seeking to engage in terrorist activity and is unlikely to have any meaningful deterrent effect on terrorist activity."[[282]](#cite_note-292) The searches were upheld by the [United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit") in *MacWade v. Kelly*.[[283]](#cite_note-293)

On April 11, 2008, MTA received a [Ferrara Fire Apparatus](/wiki/Ferrara_Fire_Apparatus "Ferrara Fire Apparatus") [Hazardous Materials Response Truck](/wiki/Dangerous_goods "Dangerous goods"), which went into service three days later. It will be used in the case of a [chemical](/wiki/Chemical_warfare "Chemical warfare") or [bioterrorist](/wiki/Bioterrorism "Bioterrorism") attack.[[284]](#cite_note-294)

[Najibullah Zazi](/wiki/Najibullah_Zazi "Najibullah Zazi") and others were arrested in September 2009 and pleaded guilty in 2010 to being part of an [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") plan to undertake suicide bombings on the New York City Subway system.[[285]](#cite_note-295)[[286]](#cite_note-296)

## Challenges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Challenges")]

### 2009–2010 budget cuts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: 2009–2010 budget cuts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/NYC_MTA_no_W.jpg/250px-NYC_MTA_no_W.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_MTA_no_W.jpg)

[28th Street](/wiki/28th_Street_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "28th Street (BMT Broadway Line)") station after the [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)") train was discontinued in mid-2010. Note the dark grey tape masked over the W bullet. (This sign has since been replaced due to the restoration of the W in 2016.)

The MTA faced a budget deficit of US$1.2 billion in 2009.[[287]](#cite_note-297) This resulted in fare increases (three times from 2008 to 2010)[[288]](#cite_note-298) and service reductions (including the elimination of two part-time subway services, the [V](/wiki/V_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "V (New York City Subway service)") and [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)")). Several other routes were modified due to the deficit. The [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)") was made a full-time local in Manhattan (in contrast to being a weekend local/weekday express before 2010), while the [Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") was extended nine stations north to [Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard](/wiki/Astoria%E2%80%93Ditmars_Boulevard_(BMT_Astoria_Line) "Astoria–Ditmars Boulevard (BMT Astoria Line)") on weekdays, both to cover the discontinued [W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)"). The [M](/wiki/M_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "M (New York City Subway service)") was combined with the [V](/wiki/V_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "V (New York City Subway service)"), routing it over the [Chrystie Street Connection](/wiki/Chrystie_Street_Connection "Chrystie Street Connection"), [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line") and [IND Queens Boulevard Line](/wiki/IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line "IND Queens Boulevard Line") to [Forest Hills–71st Avenue](/wiki/Forest_Hills%E2%80%9371st_Avenue_(IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line) "Forest Hills–71st Avenue (IND Queens Boulevard Line)") on weekdays instead of via the [BMT Fourth Avenue Line](/wiki/BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line "BMT Fourth Avenue Line") and [BMT West End Line](/wiki/BMT_West_End_Line "BMT West End Line") to Bay Parkway. The [G](/wiki/G_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "G (New York City Subway service)") was truncated to [Court Square](/wiki/Court_Square_(IND_Crosstown_Line) "Court Square (IND Crosstown Line)") full-time. Construction headways on eleven routes were lengthened, and off-peak service was lengthened on seven routes.[[289]](#cite_note-MTA_2010-299)

### 2017–2021 state of emergency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: 2017–2021 state of emergency")]

Main article: [2017–2021 New York City transit crisis](/wiki/2017%E2%80%932021_New_York_City_transit_crisis "2017–2021 New York City transit crisis")

In June 2017, Governor [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") signed an executive order declaring a state of emergency for the New York City Subway[[290]](#cite_note-300) after a series of derailments,[[291]](#cite_note-Santora2017-301)[[292]](#cite_note-Fitzsimmons2017b-302) track fires,[[293]](#cite_note-Fitzsimmons2017a-303)[[294]](#cite_note-304) and overcrowding incidents.[[293]](#cite_note-Fitzsimmons2017a-303)[[295]](#cite_note-305) On June 27, 2017, thirty-nine people were injured when an [A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)") train derailed at [125th Street](/wiki/125th_Street_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "125th Street (IND Eighth Avenue Line)"),[[296]](#cite_note-NY120172-306)[[297]](#cite_note-307) damaging tracks and signals[[291]](#cite_note-Santora2017-301) then catching fire.[[291]](#cite_note-Santora2017-301)[[298]](#cite_note-Santora-308) On July 21, 2017, the second set of wheels on a southbound [Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") train jumped the track near [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Brighton Beach (BMT Brighton Line)"), with nine people suffering injuries[[292]](#cite_note-Fitzsimmons2017b-302) due to improper maintenance of the car in question.[[299]](#cite_note-Honan-309)[[300]](#cite_note-Cook-310) To solve the system's problems, the MTA officially announced the [Genius Transit Challenge](/wiki/Genius_Transit_Challenge "Genius Transit Challenge") on June 28, where contestants could submit ideas to improve signals, communications infrastructure, or rolling stock.[[301]](#cite_note-311)[[302]](#cite_note-312)

On July 25, 2017, Chairman [Joe Lhota](/wiki/Joe_Lhota "Joe Lhota") announced a two-phase, $9 billion New York City Subway Action Plan to stabilize the subway system and to prevent the continuing decline of the system.[[303]](#cite_note-313)[[304]](#cite_note-www.mta.info-314)[[305]](#cite_note-NBC_New_York2017-315)[[306]](#cite_note-Clark2017-316) The first phase, costing $836 million, consisted of five categories of improvements in Signal and Track Maintenance, Car Reliability, System Safety and Cleanliness, Customer Communication, and Critical Management Group. The $8 billion second phase would implement the winning proposals from the Genius Transit Challenge and fix more widespread problems.[[304]](#cite_note-www.mta.info-314)[[305]](#cite_note-NBC_New_York2017-315)[[306]](#cite_note-Clark2017-316) Six winning submissions for the Genius Transit Challenge were announced in March 2018.[[307]](#cite_note-317)

In October 2017, city comptroller [Scott Stringer](/wiki/Scott_Stringer "Scott Stringer") released an analysis that subway delays could cost up to $389 million or $243.1 million or $170.2 million per year depending on the length of the delays.[[308]](#cite_note-318) In November 2017, *The New York Times* published its investigation into the crisis. It found that the crisis had arisen as a result of financially unsound decisions by local and state politicians from both the [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") and [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") parties. According to the *Times*, these decisions included overspending; overpaying unions and interest groups; advertising superficial improvement projects while ignoring more important infrastructure; and agreeing to high-interest loans that would have been unnecessary without these politicians' other interventions. By this time, the subway's 65% average on-time performance was the lowest among all major cities' transit systems, and every non-shuttle subway route's on-time performance had declined in the previous ten years.[[309]](#cite_note-319) The state of emergency ended on June 30, 2021, after previously being renewed 49 times.[[310]](#cite_note-320) As of October 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), on-time performance across all routes is at 80.6 percent.[[311]](#cite_note-321) Worsening subway reliability and service cuts in the early 2020s have been attributed to chronic mismanagement at the agency and a botched restructuring plan that was implemented under former [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_York "Governor of New York") [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo").[[312]](#cite_note-322)

### Capacity constraints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Capacity constraints")]

[![The Q train filled with commuters, many within one inch of each other. Several commuters are seen using smartphones; others are holding on to the train while standing.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Downtown_Q_Train_Rush_Hour.jpg/250px-Downtown_Q_Train_Rush_Hour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Q_Train_Rush_Hour.jpg)

The interior of a [Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") train during afternoon rush hour

Several subway lines have reached their operational limits in terms of train frequency and passengers, according to data released by the Transit Authority. By 2007, the E, L, and all [A Division](/wiki/A_Division_(New_York_City_Subway) "A Division (New York City Subway)") services except the [42nd Street Shuttle](/wiki/42nd_Street_Shuttle "42nd Street Shuttle") were beyond capacity, as well as portions of the [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)") train.[[313]](#cite_note-NYTimes-NYCS-packed-2008-323)[[314]](#cite_note-324) In April 2013, [*New York* magazine](/wiki/New_York_(magazine) "New York (magazine)") reported that the system was more crowded than it had been in the previous 66 years.[[315]](#cite_note-325) The subway reached a daily ridership of 6 million for 29 days in 2014, and was expected to record a similar ridership level for 55 days in 2015; by comparison, in 2013, daily ridership never reached 6 million.[[316]](#cite_note-326) In particular, the express tracks of the [IRT Lexington Avenue Line](/wiki/IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line "IRT Lexington Avenue Line") and [IND Queens Boulevard Line](/wiki/IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line "IND Queens Boulevard Line") are noted for operating at full capacity during peak hours.[[313]](#cite_note-NYTimes-NYCS-packed-2008-323)[[317]](#cite_note-MTA-CBTC-July2015-327) The [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") [East Side Access](/wiki/East_Side_Access "East Side Access") project, which opened in January 2023,[[318]](#cite_note-Brachfeld_2023-328) was expected to bring many more commuters to the Lexington Avenue Line.[[319]](#cite_note-329)[[320]](#cite_note-330) The [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway") was built to relieve pressure on the Lexington Avenue Line ([4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[6](/wiki/6_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6 (New York City Subway service)"), and [<6>](/wiki/6d_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6d (New York City Subway service)") trains) by shifting an estimated 225,000 passengers.[[244]](#cite_note-Daily_Intelligencer2016-254) Following the onset of the [COVID-19 pandemic in New York City](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_City "COVID-19 pandemic in New York City") in 2020, there was enough of a ridership decrease that these routes were no longer crammed to capacity during rush hours, although they still experienced some crowding.[[321]](#cite_note-Bonanos_2022_y888-331)

By early 2016, delays as a result of overcrowding were up to more than 20,000 every month, four times the amount in 2012. The overcrowded trains have resulted in an increase of assaults. With less platform space, more passengers are forced to be on the edge of the platform resulting in the increased possibility of passengers falling on the track. The MTA is considering [platform screen doors](/wiki/Platform_screen_doors "Platform screen doors"), which exist on the [AirTrain JFK](/wiki/AirTrain_JFK "AirTrain JFK") to prevent passengers falling onto the tracks.[[322]](#cite_note-Fitzsimmons2016-332) As of February 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), platform screen doors were planned to be installed in three stations, following an increase in people being pushed onto the tracks.[[259]](#cite_note-nyt-2022-02-23-269)[[323]](#cite_note-Brosnan_2022-333)

#### Expanding service frequency via CBTC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Expanding service frequency via CBTC")]

Further information: [Signaling of the New York City Subway § Automation](/wiki/Signaling_of_the_New_York_City_Subway#Automation "Signaling of the New York City Subway")

The MTA has sought to relieve overcrowding by upgrading signaling systems on some lines to use communications-based train control.[[324]](#cite_note-334) CBTC installation on the [Flushing Line](/wiki/IRT_Flushing_Line "IRT Flushing Line") is expected to increase the rate of trains per hour on the [7](/wiki/7_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7 (New York City Subway service)") and [<7>](/wiki/7d_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7d (New York City Subway service)")​ trains, but little relief will come to other crowded lines until later.[[189]](#cite_note-mta.info2016-199) The [L](/wiki/L_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "L (New York City Subway service)") train, which is overcrowded during rush hours, already has CBTC operation.[[325]](#cite_note-chan-January14-335) The installation of CBTC has reduced the L's running time by 3%.[[244]](#cite_note-Daily_Intelligencer2016-254) Even with CBTC, there are limits on the potential increased service. For L service to be increased further, a power upgrade as well as additional space for the L to turn around at its Manhattan terminus, [Eighth Avenue](/wiki/14th_Street%E2%80%93Eighth_Avenue_(New_York_City_Subway) "14th Street–Eighth Avenue (New York City Subway)"), are needed.[[125]](#cite_note-Tangel-132)

#### Service frequency and car capacity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Service frequency and car capacity")]

Due to an increase of ridership, the MTA has tried to increase capacity wherever possible by adding more frequent service, specifically during the evening hours. This increase is not likely to keep up with the growth of subway ridership.[[322]](#cite_note-Fitzsimmons2016-332)[[326]](#cite_note-336)[[327]](#cite_note-337) Some lines have capacity for additional trains during peak times, but there are too few subway cars for this additional service to be operated.[[125]](#cite_note-Tangel-132)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/R211T_soft_shell_open_gangway.jpg/250px-R211T_soft_shell_open_gangway.jpg)](/wiki/File:R211T_soft_shell_open_gangway.jpg)

View of an open-gangway used on the R211T subway car

As part of the [R211](/wiki/R211_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R211 (New York City Subway car)") subway car order, the MTA is planning to test a train of 20 [open-gangway](/wiki/Articulated_train "Articulated train") experimental prototype cars, which could increase capacity by up to 10% by utilizing space between cars. The order could be expanded to include up to 750 open-gangway cars.[[328]](#cite_note-MTACapital-15-19-2015-338)[[329]](#cite_note-MTA-CapitalOversight-Jan2016-339)[[330]](#cite_note-340) On December 16, 2024, the MTA Board voted to approve the second option order of R211s, which would contain 80 additional open-gangway trains.[[331]](#cite_note-Spivack_w509-341)[[332]](#cite_note-Russo-Lennon_i775-342)

#### Platform crowd control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Platform crowd control")]

The MTA is also testing smaller ideas on some services. Starting in late 2015, 100 "station platform controllers" were deployed for the [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)"), [6](/wiki/6_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6 (New York City Subway service)"), and [7](/wiki/7_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7 (New York City Subway service)") trains, to manage the flow of passengers on and off crowded trains during morning rush hours. There were a total of 129 such employees, who also answer passengers' questions about subway directions, rather than having conductors answer them and thus delaying the trains.[[333]](#cite_note-343)[[334]](#cite_note-344)[[335]](#cite_note-345)[[336]](#cite_note-Harshbarger2015-346) In early 2017, the test was expanded to the afternoon peak period with an increase of 35 platform conductors.[[189]](#cite_note-mta.info2016-199)[[337]](#cite_note-347) In November of the same year, 140 platform controllers and 90 conductors gained [iPhone 6S](/wiki/IPhone_6S "IPhone 6S") devices so they could receive notifications of, and tell riders about, subway disruptions.[[338]](#cite_note-348) Subway guards, the predecessors to the platform controllers, were first used during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[322]](#cite_note-Fitzsimmons2016-332)

Shortened "next stop" announcements on trains were being tested on the [2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)") and [5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)") trains. "Step aside" signs on the platforms, reminding boarding passengers to let departing passengers off the train first, were tested at [Grand Central–42nd Street](/wiki/Grand_Central%E2%80%9342nd_Street_(IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line) "Grand Central–42nd Street (IRT Lexington Avenue Line)"), [51st Street](/wiki/51st_Street_(IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line) "51st Street (IRT Lexington Avenue Line)"), and [86th Street](/wiki/86th_Street_(IRT_Lexington_Avenue_Line) "86th Street (IRT Lexington Avenue Line)") on the Lexington Avenue Line.[[336]](#cite_note-Harshbarger2015-346)[[339]](#cite_note-349) Cameras would also be installed so the MTA could observe passenger overcrowding.[[244]](#cite_note-Daily_Intelligencer2016-254)[[340]](#cite_note-350)[[341]](#cite_note-Scribd_2015-351)

In systems like the [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground"), stations are simply closed off when they are overcrowded; that type of restriction is not necessary yet on the New York City Subway, according to MTA spokesman Kevin Ortiz.[[322]](#cite_note-Fitzsimmons2016-332)

### Subway flooding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Subway flooding")]

[![Rain from drainage pipes entering a subway car](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/New_York_City_Subway_100_1888_edited.JPG/250px-New_York_City_Subway_100_1888_edited.JPG)](/wiki/File:New_York_City_Subway_100_1888_edited.JPG)

Rain from [drainage](/wiki/Drainage "Drainage") pipes entering a subway car

Service on the subway system is occasionally disrupted by flooding from rainstorms, even minor ones.[[342]](#cite_note-Mancini_2010-352) Rainwater can disrupt signals underground and require the electrified [third rail](/wiki/Third_rail "Third rail") to be shut off. As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), the MTA moves 13 million US gallons (49 million litres) of water daily when it is not raining.[[343]](#cite_note-Subway_Flooding-353) The pumps and drainage system can handle a rainfall rate of 1.75 inches (44 mm) per hour.[[343]](#cite_note-Subway_Flooding-353)[[344]](#cite_note-flood_report-354): 10 Since 1992, $357 million has been used to improve 269 pump rooms. By August 2007, $115 million was earmarked to upgrade the remaining 18 pump rooms.[[345]](#cite_note-355)

Despite these improvements, the transit system continues to experience flooding problems. On August 8, 2007, after more than 3 inches (76 mm) of rain fell within an hour, the subway system flooded, causing almost every subway service to either be disabled or seriously disrupted, effectively halting the morning rush.[[346]](#cite_note-356) On September 1, 2021, when 3 to 5 inches (76 to 127 mm) of rain per hour fell during [Hurricane Ida](/wiki/Hurricane_Ida "Hurricane Ida"), service on the entire subway system was suspended.[[347]](#cite_note-357)

As part of a $130 million and an estimated 18-month project, the MTA began installing new subway grates in September 2008 in an attempt to prevent rain from overflowing into the subway system. The metallic structures, designed with the help of architectural firms and meant as a piece of public art, are placed atop existing grates but with a 3-to-4-inch (76 to 102 mm) sleeve to prevent debris and rain from flooding the subway. The racks will at first be installed in the three most flood-prone areas as determined by hydrologists: [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica,_Queens "Jamaica, Queens"), [Tribeca](/wiki/Tribeca "Tribeca"), and the [Upper West Side](/wiki/Upper_West_Side "Upper West Side"). Each neighborhood has its own distinct design, some featuring a wave-like deck which increases in height and features seating (as in Jamaica), others with a flatter deck that includes seating and a bike rack.[[348]](#cite_note-358)[[349]](#cite_note-359)[[350]](#cite_note-360)

In October 2012, [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") caused [significant damage to New York City](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy_in_New_York "Hurricane Sandy in New York"), and many subway tunnels were inundated with floodwater. The subway opened with limited service two days after the storm and was running at 80 percent capacity within five days; some infrastructure needed years to repair. A year after the storm, MTA spokesperson Kevin Ortiz said, "This was unprecedented in terms of the amount of damage that we were seeing throughout the system."[[351]](#cite_note-361)[[352]](#cite_note-Cox-362) The storm flooded nine of the system's 14 underwater tunnels, many subway lines, and several subway yards, as well as completely destroying a portion of the [IND Rockaway Line](/wiki/IND_Rockaway_Line "IND Rockaway Line") and much of the [South Ferry](/wiki/South_Ferry_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "South Ferry (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") terminal station. Reconstruction required many partial or total closures on several lines and tunnels.[[353]](#cite_note-363) Heavy flooding also occurred in September 2021 during [Hurricane Ida](/wiki/Hurricane_Ida "Hurricane Ida")[[354]](#cite_note-NPR_2021_m246-364) and [in September 2023](/wiki/September_2023_New_York_floods "September 2023 New York floods") during the aftermath of [Tropical Storm Ophelia](/wiki/Tropical_Storm_Ophelia_(2023) "Tropical Storm Ophelia (2023)").[[355]](#cite_note-365)

### Full and partial subway closures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Full and partial subway closures")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/MTA_New_York_City_Transit_Sanitizes_Stations_and_Subway_Cars_%2849618677077%29.jpg/250px-MTA_New_York_City_Transit_Sanitizes_Stations_and_Subway_Cars_%2849618677077%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:MTA_New_York_City_Transit_Sanitizes_Stations_and_Subway_Cars_(49618677077).jpg)

Disinfection of New York City Subway cars against [coronavirus](/wiki/Severe_acute_respiratory_syndrome_coronavirus_2 "Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2") during the [COVID-19 pandemic in New York City](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_City "COVID-19 pandemic in New York City")

Before 2011, there have been some full subway closures for transit strikes ([January 1–13, 1966](/wiki/1966_New_York_City_transit_strike "1966 New York City transit strike"),[[356]](#cite_note-366) [April 1–11, 1980](/wiki/1980_New_York_City_transit_strike "1980 New York City transit strike"),[[357]](#cite_note-367) [December 20–22, 2005](/wiki/2005_New_York_City_transit_strike "2005 New York City transit strike"))[[358]](#cite_note-368) and blackouts ([November 9–10, 1965](/wiki/Northeast_blackout_of_1965 "Northeast blackout of 1965"),[[359]](#cite_note-369) [July 13–14, 1977](/wiki/New_York_City_blackout_of_1977 "New York City blackout of 1977"),[[360]](#cite_note-370) [August 14–16, 2003](/wiki/Northeast_blackout_of_2003 "Northeast blackout of 2003")[[361]](#cite_note-371)).

On August 27, 2011, due to the approach of [Hurricane Irene](/wiki/Hurricane_Irene_(2011) "Hurricane Irene (2011)"), the MTA suspended subway service at noon in anticipation of heavy flooding on tracks and in tunnels. It was the first weather-caused shutdown in the history of the system.[[362]](#cite_note-372) Service was restored by August 29.[[363]](#cite_note-373)[[364]](#cite_note-374)

On October 29, 2012, a full closure was ordered before the arrival of [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy").[[352]](#cite_note-Cox-362) All services on the subway, the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") and [Metro-North](/wiki/Metro-North "Metro-North") were gradually shut down by 7:00 P.M. to protect passengers, employees, and equipment from the coming storm.[[365]](#cite_note-375) The storm [caused serious damage](/wiki/Effects_of_Hurricane_Sandy_in_New_York "Effects of Hurricane Sandy in New York") to the system, especially the [IND Rockaway Line](/wiki/IND_Rockaway_Line "IND Rockaway Line"), upon which many sections between [Howard Beach–JFK Airport](/wiki/Howard_Beach%E2%80%93JFK_Airport_(IND_Rockaway_Line) "Howard Beach–JFK Airport (IND Rockaway Line)") and [Hammels Wye](/wiki/Hammels_Wye "Hammels Wye") on the [Rockaway Peninsula](/wiki/Rockaway,_Queens "Rockaway, Queens") were heavily damaged, leaving it essentially isolated from the rest of the system.[[366]](#cite_note-asandy-376)[[367]](#cite_note-377) This required the NYCTA to truck in 20 [R32](/wiki/R32_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R32 (New York City Subway car)") subway cars to the line to provide some interim service (temporarily designated the [H](/wiki/H_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "H (New York City Subway service)")).[[368]](#cite_note-378)[[369]](#cite_note-SandyRecoveryMap-379)[[370]](#cite_note-380) Also, several of the system's tunnels under the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") were flooded by the storm surge.[[371]](#cite_note-381) [South Ferry](/wiki/South_Ferry_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "South Ferry (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") suffered serious water damage and did not reopen until April 4, 2013, by restoring service to the older [loop-configured station](/wiki/South_Ferry_loops "South Ferry loops") that had been replaced in 2009;[[372]](#cite_note-Mann_2013-382)[[373]](#cite_note-383) the stub-end terminal tracks remained out of service until June 2017.[[374]](#cite_note-384)[[375]](#cite_note-385)[[376]](#cite_note-bidprocessouthferry-386)[[377]](#cite_note-387)

Since 2015, there have been several blizzard-related subway shutdowns. On January 26, 2015, another full closure was ordered by [New York Governor](/wiki/New_York_Governor "New York Governor") [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") due to the [January 2015 nor'easter](/wiki/January_2015_nor%27easter "January 2015 nor'easter"), originally projected to leave New York City with 20 to 30 inches (51 to 76 cm) of snow; this was the first shutdown in the system's history to be ordered due to snow.[[378]](#cite_note-388) The next day, the subway system was partially reopened.[[379]](#cite_note-389)[[380]](#cite_note-390) Several residents criticized the decision to shut down the subway system due to snow, as the nor'easter dropped much less snow in the city than originally expected, totaling only 9.8 inches (25 cm) in [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park").[[381]](#cite_note-controversy-391)[[382]](#cite_note-392) For subsequent snowstorms, the MTA published a winter underground-only subway service plan. When this plan is in effect, all above-ground stations would be closed and all above-ground service suspended, except at [125th Street and Broadway](/wiki/125th_Street_station_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "125th Street station (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)"), where [1](/wiki/1_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "1 (New York City Subway service)") trains would run above ground but skip the station. Underground service would remain operational, except at a small number of stations that would be closed because of their proximity to above-ground portions of the system.[[383]](#cite_note-393) This plan was first used on January 23, 2016, during the [January 2016 United States blizzard](/wiki/January_2016_United_States_blizzard "January 2016 United States blizzard");[[384]](#cite_note-394) it was also used on March 14, 2017, due to the [March 2017 nor'easter](/wiki/March_2017_nor%27easter "March 2017 nor'easter").[[385]](#cite_note-395) On August 4, 2020, service at above-ground stations was suspended due to the high wind gusts brought by [Tropical Storm Isaias](/wiki/Hurricane_Isaias "Hurricane Isaias").[[386]](#cite_note-396) As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit), the underground-only service pattern is no longer used during blizzards and snowstorms.[[387]](#cite_note-397)

Starting on May 6, 2020, as a result of the [COVID-19 pandemic in New York City](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_City "COVID-19 pandemic in New York City"), stations were closed between 1:00 a.m. and 5:00 a.m. for cleaning and disinfecting.[[388]](#cite_note-398)[[389]](#cite_note-399) Nevertheless, over 500 trains continued running every 20 minutes between 1 a.m. and 5 a.m., carrying only transit workers and emergency personnel. The trains kept running because there was not enough space in the system to store all trains simultaneously, and also so that they could easily resume service upon the start of rush hour at 5 a.m.[[390]](#cite_note-400) In February 2021, the overnight closures were shortened to between 2 and 4 a.m.,[[391]](#cite_note-401)[[392]](#cite_note-402) and in May 2021, Cuomo announced that 24-hour service would resume on May 17.[[393]](#cite_note-Slattery_Sommerfeldt_Guse_2021-403)[[394]](#cite_note-404) This was the longest shutdown in the subway's history.[[395]](#cite_note-405)

### Litter and rodents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Litter and rodents")]

Further information: [Rats in New York City](/wiki/Rats_in_New_York_City "Rats in New York City")

Litter accumulation in the subway system is perennial. In the 1970s and 1980s, dirty trains and platforms, as well as graffiti, were a serious problem. The situation had improved since then, but the 2010 budget crisis, which caused over 100 of the cleaning staff to lose their jobs, threatened to curtail trash removal.[[396]](#cite_note-406)[[397]](#cite_note-407) Every day, the MTA removes 40 tons of trash from 3,500 trash receptacles.[[398]](#cite_note-Barone2017-408)

The New York City Subway system is infested with [rats](/wiki/Rat "Rat").[[399]](#cite_note-409) Rats are sometimes seen on platforms,[[400]](#cite_note-410) and are commonly seen foraging through garbage thrown onto the tracks. They are believed to pose a health hazard, and on rare instances have been known to bite humans.[[401]](#cite_note-411) Subway stations notorious for rat infestation include [Chambers Street](/wiki/Chambers_Street_%E2%80%93_World_Trade_Center_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "Chambers Street – World Trade Center (IND Eighth Avenue Line)"), [Jay Street–MetroTech](/wiki/Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_(IND_Fulton_Street_Line) "Jay Street–MetroTech (IND Fulton Street Line)"), [West Fourth Street](/wiki/West_Fourth_Street_%E2%80%93_Washington_Square_(New_York_City_Subway) "West Fourth Street – Washington Square (New York City Subway)"), [Spring Street](/wiki/Spring_Street_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "Spring Street (IND Eighth Avenue Line)") and [145th Street](/wiki/145th_Street_(IRT_Lenox_Avenue_Line) "145th Street (IRT Lenox Avenue Line)").[[402]](#cite_note-412)

Decades of efforts to eradicate or simply thin the rat population in the system have been a failure. In March 2009, the Transit Authority announced a series of changes to its vermin control strategy, including new [poison](/wiki/Rodenticide "Rodenticide") formulas and experimental trap designs.[[403]](#cite_note-413) In October 2011, they announced a new initiative to clean 25 subway stations, along with their garbage rooms, of rat infestations.[[404]](#cite_note-414) That same month, the MTA announced a pilot program aimed at reducing levels of garbage in the subways by removing all garbage bins from the subway platforms. The initiative was tested at the [Eighth Street–New York University](/wiki/Eighth_Street%E2%80%93New_York_University_(BMT_Broadway_Line) "Eighth Street–New York University (BMT Broadway Line)") and [Flushing–Main Street](/wiki/Flushing%E2%80%93Main_Street_(IRT_Flushing_Line) "Flushing–Main Street (IRT Flushing Line)") stations.[[405]](#cite_note-415) As of March 2016, stations along the [BMT Jamaica Line](/wiki/BMT_Jamaica_Line "BMT Jamaica Line"), [BMT Myrtle Avenue Line](/wiki/BMT_Myrtle_Avenue_Line "BMT Myrtle Avenue Line"), and various other stations had their garbage cans removed due to the success of the program.[[406]](#cite_note-www.mta.info2-416) In March 2017 the program was ended as a failure.[[407]](#cite_note-417)

The old vacuum trains that are designed to remove trash from the tracks are ineffective and often broken.[[406]](#cite_note-www.mta.info2-416) A 2016 study by Travel Math had the New York City Subway listed as the dirtiest subway system in the country based on the number of viable bacteria cells.[[408]](#cite_note-418) In August 2016, the MTA announced that it had initiated Operation Track Sweep, an aggressive plan to dramatically reduce the amount of trash on the tracks and in the subway environment. This was expected to reduce track fires and train delays. As part of the plan, the frequency of station track cleaning would be increased, and 94 stations would be cleaned per two-week period, an increase from the previous rate of 34 stations every two weeks.[[398]](#cite_note-Barone2017-408) The MTA launched an intensive two-week, system-wide cleaning on September 12, 2016.[[409]](#cite_note-419) Several vacuum trains were delivered in 2018 and 2019.[[410]](#cite_note-420) The operation planned to also include 27 new refuse cars.[[411]](#cite_note-421)
