### Noise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Noise")]

Rolling stock on the New York City Subway produces high levels of noise that exceed guidelines set by the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") and the [U.S. Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/U.S._Environmental_Protection_Agency "U.S. Environmental Protection Agency").[[412]](#cite_note-ScienceDaily_2016-422) In 2006, [Columbia University's Mailman School of Public Health](/wiki/Columbia_Mailman_School_of_Public_Health "Columbia Mailman School of Public Health") found noise levels averaged 95 [decibel](/wiki/Decibel "Decibel") (dB) inside subway cars and 94 dB on platforms.[[412]](#cite_note-ScienceDaily_2016-422) Daily exposure to noise at such levels for as little as 30 minutes can lead to hearing loss.[[412]](#cite_note-ScienceDaily_2016-422) Noise on one in 10 platforms exceeded 100 dB.[[412]](#cite_note-ScienceDaily_2016-422) Under WHO and EPA guidelines, noise exposure at that level is limited to 1.5 minutes.[[412]](#cite_note-ScienceDaily_2016-422) A subsequent study by Columbia and the [University of Washington](/wiki/University_of_Washington "University of Washington") found higher average noise levels in the subway (80.4 dB) than on commuter trains including [Port Authority Trans-Hudson](/wiki/Port_Authority_Trans-Hudson "Port Authority Trans-Hudson") (PATH) (79.4 dB), [Metro-North](/wiki/Metro-North "Metro-North") (75.1 dB) and [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") (LIRR) (74.9 dB).[[413]](#cite_note-Internet_Archive_2010-423) Since the decibel scale is a [logarithmic scale](/wiki/Logarithmic_scale "Logarithmic scale"), sound at 95 dB is 10 times more intense than at 85 dB, 100 times more intense than at 75 dB, and so forth.[[413]](#cite_note-Internet_Archive_2010-423) In the second study, peak subway noise registered at 102.1 dB.[[413]](#cite_note-Internet_Archive_2010-423)

For the construction of the Second Avenue Subway, the MTA, with the engineering firm Arup, worked to reduce the noise levels in stations. In order to reduce noise for all future stations starting with the Second Avenue Subway, the MTA is investing in low-vibration track using ties encased in concrete-covered rubber and neoprene pads. Continuously welded rail, which is also being installed, reduces the noise being made by the wheels of trains. The biggest change that is going to be made is in the design of stations. Current stations were built with tile and stone, which bounce sound everywhere, while newer stations will have the ceilings lined with absorbent fiberglass or mineral wool that will direct sound toward the train and not the platform. With less noise from the trains, platform announcements could be heard more clearly. They will be clearer with speakers spaced periodically on the platform, angled so that announcements can be heard by the riders. The Second Avenue Subway has the first stations to test this technology.[[414]](#cite_note-Marshall_2016-424)

### Air quality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Air quality")]

Air quality in the New York City Subway is a recurring concern. A study from 2023 found much higher exposure to [particulate matter](/wiki/Particulate_matter "Particulate matter") concentrations in underground platforms than above ground.[[415]](#cite_note-425) Another study from 2014 found that concentrations of particulate matter underground are higher than at street level.[[416]](#cite_note-426)

## Public relations and cultural impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Public relations and cultural impact")]

### Entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Entertainment")]

Main article: [Music Under New York](/wiki/Music_Under_New_York "Music Under New York")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Subway_musicians_%286337954877%29.jpg/250px-Subway_musicians_%286337954877%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Subway_musicians_(6337954877).jpg)

Musicians perform in the [Delancey Street/Essex Street station](/wiki/Delancey_Street/Essex_Street_station "Delancey Street/Essex Street station") in 2011.

The subway is a popular venue for [busking](/wiki/Street_performance "Street performance"). A permit is not required to perform, but certain codes of conduct are required.[[417]](#cite_note-MTA-performance-427) Some buskers are affiliated with [Music Under New York](/wiki/Music_Under_New_York "Music Under New York") (MUNY), a part of the [Arts & Design](/wiki/MTA_Arts_%26_Design "MTA Arts & Design") program by the MTA. Since 1987, MTA has sponsored the MUNY program[[418]](#cite_note-428) in which street musicians enter a competitive contest to be assigned to the preferred high traffic locations. Each year, applications are reviewed and approximately 70 eligible performers are selected and contacted to participate in live auditions held for one day.[[419]](#cite_note-429)[[420]](#cite_note-430)[[421]](#cite_note-431)[[422]](#cite_note-432)

### Miss Subways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Miss Subways")]

Main article: [Miss Subways](/wiki/Miss_Subways "Miss Subways")

[![An advertisement for Miss Subways at the New York Transit Museum](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/NYC_Transit_Museum_Miss_Subways.jpg/250px-NYC_Transit_Museum_Miss_Subways.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_Transit_Museum_Miss_Subways.jpg)

An advertisement for Miss Subways at the [New York Transit Museum](/wiki/New_York_Transit_Museum "New York Transit Museum")

From 1941 to 1976, the Board of Transportation/[New York City Transit Authority](/wiki/New_York_City_Transit_Authority "New York City Transit Authority") sponsored the "Miss Subways" publicity campaign.[[423]](#cite_note-433) In the musical *[On the Town](/wiki/On_the_Town_(musical) "On the Town (musical)")*, the character Miss Turnstiles is based on the Miss Subways campaign.[[424]](#cite_note-434)[[425]](#cite_note-435) The campaign was resurrected in 2004, for one year, as "Ms. Subways". It was part of the 100th anniversary celebrations. The monthly campaign, which included the winners' photos and biographical blurbs on placards in subway cars, featured such winners as [Mona Freeman](/wiki/Mona_Freeman "Mona Freeman") and prominent New York City restaurateur [Ellen Goodman](/wiki/Ellen_Goodman "Ellen Goodman"). The winner of this contest was Caroline Sanchez-Bernat, an actress from [Morningside Heights](/wiki/Morningside_Heights "Morningside Heights").[[426]](#cite_note-436)

### Subway Series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Subway Series")]

Main article: [Subway Series](/wiki/Subway_Series "Subway Series")

Subway Series is a term applied to any series of baseball games between New York City teams, as opposing teams can travel to compete merely by using the subway system. Subway Series is a term long used in New York, going back to series between the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") or [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants_(NL) "New York Giants (NL)") and the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") in the 1940s and 1950s. Today, the term is used to describe the [rivalry](/wiki/Mets%E2%80%93Yankees_rivalry "Mets–Yankees rivalry") between the Yankees and the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets"). During the [2000 World Series](/wiki/2000_World_Series "2000 World Series"), cars on the [4 train](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)") (which stopped at [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium_(1923) "Yankee Stadium (1923)")) were painted with Yankee colors, while cars on the [7 train](/wiki/7_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7 (New York City Subway service)") (which stopped at [Shea Stadium](/wiki/Shea_Stadium "Shea Stadium")) had Mets colors.[[427]](#cite_note-437)

### Holiday Nostalgia Train

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Holiday Nostalgia Train")]

[![Holiday Nostalgia Train at Second Avenue station in December 2016](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Nostalgia_train_2nd_Av_a_jeh.jpg/250px-Nostalgia_train_2nd_Av_a_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nostalgia_train_2nd_Av_a_jeh.jpg)

Holiday Nostalgia Train at [Second Avenue](/wiki/Second_Avenue_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "Second Avenue (IND Sixth Avenue Line)") station in 2016

[![Holiday Nostalgia Train at 125th Street in 2022](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/2022_Holiday_Train.jpg/250px-2022_Holiday_Train.jpg)](/wiki/File:2022_Holiday_Train.jpg)

Holiday Train in December 2022 at [125th Street](/wiki/125th_Street_station_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "125th Street station (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") station

Since 2003, the MTA has operated a Holiday Nostalgia Train on Sundays in November and December, from the first Sunday after Thanksgiving to the Sunday before Christmas Day,[[428]](#cite_note-438) except in 2011 and 2023, when the train operated on Saturdays instead of Sundays.[[429]](#cite_note-439) This train is made of vintage cars from the [R1–9 fleet](/wiki/R1%E2%80%939_fleet "R1–9 fleet"), which have been preserved by Railway Preservation Corp. and the [New York Transit Museum](/wiki/New_York_Transit_Museum "New York Transit Museum"). Until 2017, the train made all stops between [Second Avenue](/wiki/Second_Avenue_station "Second Avenue station") in Manhattan and [Queens Plaza](/wiki/Queens_Plaza_station "Queens Plaza station") in Queens via the [IND Sixth Avenue Line](/wiki/IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line "IND Sixth Avenue Line") and the [IND Queens Boulevard Line](/wiki/IND_Queens_Boulevard_Line "IND Queens Boulevard Line"). In 2017, the train ran between [Second Avenue](/wiki/Second_Avenue_(IND_Sixth_Avenue_Line) "Second Avenue (IND Sixth Avenue Line)") and [96th Street](/wiki/96th_Street_(Second_Avenue_Subway) "96th Street (Second Avenue Subway)") via the newly opened [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway").[[430]](#cite_note-440) Between 2018 and 2019 and in 2023, the northern terminal was located at [145th Street](/wiki/145th_Street_station_(IND_lines) "145th Street station (IND lines)"). In 2020, the holiday train was done virtually due to the Covid-19 pandemic. In 2021 and January 2022, the holiday train was ran as the [R32](/wiki/R32_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R32 (New York City Subway car)") retirement run; during the first three weeks, it ran Second Avenue to 145th Street as a [D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)") train, and during the final week, it ran from 96th Street-2nd Avenue to [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach_station_(BMT_Brighton_Line) "Brighton Beach station (BMT Brighton Line)"). In 2022, the Holiday Train ran on IRT tracks with the [Train of Many Colors](/wiki/Train_of_Many_Colors "Train of Many Colors"), signed as the [1](/wiki/1_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "1 (New York City Subway service)") train, which traveled between [137th Street](/wiki/137th_Street%E2%80%93City_College_station "137th Street–City College station") and [Chambers Street](/wiki/Chambers_Street_station_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "Chambers Street station (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)").[[431]](#cite_note-441) Since 2024, the train's northern terminal was moved back to 96th Street–Second Avenue.[[432]](#cite_note-442)

The contract, car numbers (and year built) used generally comprises [R1](/wiki/R1_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R1 (New York City Subway car)") 100 (built 1930), [R1](/wiki/R1_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R1 (New York City Subway car)") 103 (1930), [R1](/wiki/R1_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R1 (New York City Subway car)") 381 (1931), [R4](/wiki/R4_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R4 (New York City Subway car)") 401 (1932), [R4](/wiki/R4_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R4 (New York City Subway car)") 484 (1932) – Bulls Eye lighting and a test P.A. system added in 1946, [R6](/wiki/R6_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R6 (New York City Subway car)")-3 1000 (1935), [R6](/wiki/R6_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R6 (New York City Subway car)")-1 1300 (1937), [R7A](/wiki/R7A_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R7A (New York City Subway car)") 1575 (1938) – rebuilt in 1947 as a prototype for the [R10](/wiki/R10_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R10 (New York City Subway car)") subway car, and [R9](/wiki/R9_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R9 (New York City Subway car)") 1802 (1940).[[433]](#cite_note-443)

### Full train wraps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Full train wraps")]

Since 2008, the MTA has tested full train wraps on [42nd Street Shuttle](/wiki/42nd_Street_Shuttle "42nd Street Shuttle") rolling stock. In full train wraps, advertising entirely covers the interiors and exteriors of the train, as opposed to other routes, whose stock generally only displays advertising on placards inside the train.[[434]](#cite_note-444)[[435]](#cite_note-445) While most advertisements are well received, a few advertisements have been controversial. Among the more contentious wraps that were withdrawn are a 2015 ad for the TV show *[The Man in the High Castle](/wiki/The_Man_in_the_High_Castle_(TV_series) "The Man in the High Castle (TV series)")*, which featured a [Nazi flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Germany#Nazi_Germany "Flag of Germany"),[[436]](#cite_note-446)[[437]](#cite_note-447) and an ad for [Fox Sports 1](/wiki/Fox_Sports_1 "Fox Sports 1"), in which a shuttle train and half of its seats were plastered with negative quotes about the [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks"), one of the city's [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") teams.[[438]](#cite_note-448)[[439]](#cite_note-449)

Other routes have seen limited implementation of full train wraps. For instance, in 2010, one [R142A](/wiki/R142A_(New_York_City_Subway_car) "R142A (New York City Subway car)") train set on the [6](/wiki/6_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "6 (New York City Subway service)") route was wrapped with a [Target](/wiki/Target_Corporation "Target Corporation") advertisement.[[440]](#cite_note-450) In 2014, the [Jaguar F-Type](/wiki/Jaguar_F-Type "Jaguar F-Type") was advertised on train sets running on the [F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)") route.[[441]](#cite_note-451)[[442]](#cite_note-452) Some of these wraps have also been controversial, such as a [Lane Bryant](/wiki/Lane_Bryant "Lane Bryant") wrap in 2015 that displayed lingerie models on the exteriors of train cars.[[443]](#cite_note-453)

### LGBT Pride-themed trains and MetroCards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: LGBT Pride-themed trains and MetroCards")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/MTA_Pride_Month_MetroCard_for_NYC_Subway.jpg/250px-MTA_Pride_Month_MetroCard_for_NYC_Subway.jpg)](/wiki/File:MTA_Pride_Month_MetroCard_for_NYC_Subway.jpg)

MetroCard for Pride Month in June 2019

The New York City Subway system commemorates [Pride Month](/wiki/LGBT_Pride "LGBT Pride") in June with Pride-themed posters.[[444]](#cite_note-454) The MTA celebrated [Stonewall 50 – WorldPride NYC 2019](/wiki/Stonewall_50_%E2%80%93_WorldPride_NYC_2019 "Stonewall 50 – WorldPride NYC 2019") in June 2019 with rainbow-themed Pride logos on the subway trains, as well as Pride-themed MetroCards.[[445]](#cite_note-455) In 2023, some trains were given different pride hearts, which were also installed on both the [Long Island Railroad](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") and [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad").[[446]](#cite_note-456) The MTA also released another set of Pride MetroCards.[[447]](#cite_note-457)

### Guerrilla art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Guerrilla art")]

See also: [Graffiti in New York City](/wiki/Graffiti_in_New_York_City "Graffiti in New York City")

The New York City Subway system has been a target for unauthorized or ["guerrilla"](/wiki/Guerrilla_art "Guerrilla art") art since the 1970s, beginning with graffiti and tagging. Originally thought of as vandalism, the art form eventually emerged as an authoritative typology in the 1980s, especially with the release of the 1983 documentary *[Style Wars](/wiki/Style_Wars "Style Wars")*. Prominent pop-artist [Keith Haring](/wiki/Keith_Haring "Keith Haring") got his start tagging blank billboards on subway platforms with chalk art.[[448]](#cite_note-458) In 2019–2020, the [Bronx Museum](/wiki/Bronx_Museum "Bronx Museum") mounted an exhibition of graffiti-tagged subway cars.[[449]](#cite_note-459)

More contemporary installations have taken place as well. In 2014, artist London Kaye [yarn-bombed](/wiki/Yarnbombing "Yarnbombing") the L train, wrapping metal hand poles in knit fabric.[[450]](#cite_note-460) In 2019, artist Ian Callender used projectors to show accurate views of the cityscape above moving 6 trains on the ceilings of entire cars.[[451]](#cite_note-461) In 2021, illustrator Devon Rodriguez went viral for his drawings of fellow commuters.[[452]](#cite_note-462)

### No Pants Ride

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_City_Subway&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: No Pants Ride")]

Further information: [No Pants Subway Ride](/wiki/No_Pants_Subway_Ride "No Pants Subway Ride")

In 2002, the New York City Subway began hosting an event called the [No Pants Subway Ride](/wiki/No_Pants_Subway_Ride "No Pants Subway Ride"), where people ride the subway without their pants. The event is typically held each January but has not been held since 2020 due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_City "COVID-19 pandemic in New York City").[[453]](#cite_note-463)
